{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Nachikatsuura, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = {{Nihongo|Kumano Nachi Taisha|熊野那智大社}} | image = Kumano-Nachi-shrine002.JPG | caption = Kumano Nachi Taisha's ''[[Heiden (Shinto)|heiden]]'' | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|33|40|07|N|135|53|26|E|display=title,inline|region:JP_type:landmark_source:dewiki}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Fusumi no kami (Izanami no mikoto) and others<ref>Cali, Joseph. Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion. — Honolulu, 2013. — pp. 253-256.</ref> | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = [[Nachikatsuura]], [[Higashimuro District, Wakayama|Higashimuro District]], [[Wakayama Prefecture]], [[Japan]] | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }}
{{Nihongo|'''Kumano Nachi Taisha'''|熊野那智大社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] and part of the [[UNESCO]]-designated [[World Heritage]] [[Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range]] of [[Japan]]. The [[Kumano Kodō]] route connects it to other sites under the same classification, which are primarily located in [[Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan. The four sites on the route, classified as pilgrimage destinations and World Heritage Sites, are: 1) Nachi Taisha; 2) [[Kumano Hongū Taisha|Hongū Taisha]]; 3) [[Kumano Hayatama Taisha|Hayatama Taisha]]; 4) [[Mount Kōya|Koya-san]].
Kumano Nachi Taisha is also one of the three sacred [[Kumano Shrine|Kumano Sanzan]] shrines: # Kumano Nachi Taisha # [[Kumano Hongū Taisha]] # [[Kumano Hayatama Taisha]] This classification is based mostly in Japanese history, as pilgrims would travel to all three sites to complete their pilgrimage.
Kumano Nachi Taisha is an example of Buddhist and Shinto [[syncretism]] ([[Shinbutsu shūgō]]) nestled in the Kii Mountains, near Kii Katsuura, Japan. Cedar forests surround the site. The 133-meter [[Nachi Falls|Nachi Waterfall]], worshiped at the Hiryū Shrine near Kumano Nachi Taisha, is believed to be inhabited by a ''[[kami]]'' called Hiryū [[Gongen]].<ref name="nachi">{{cite web|url=http://www.kkr.mlit.go.jp/wakayama/rekishi/disco131.html|title=Hiryū Gongen|last=Kamizaka|first=Jirō|publisher=Ministry of Land, Infrastructure and Transport - Kinki Regional Development Bureau|language=ja|access-date=28 March 2010|archive-date=23 July 2011|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110723162553/http://www.kkr.mlit.go.jp/wakayama/rekishi/disco131.html|url-status=dead}}</ref> Also, there is a sacred tree at this site, the Sacred [[Camphor laurel|Camphor Tree]], located between the Nachi Shrine (''[[Heiden (Shinto)|heiden]]'') and [[Seiganto-ji|Seigantoji temple]]. It is 850 years old and is said to have been planted by [[Taira no Shigemori|Taira-no-Shigemori]] (1138–1179). The straw rope (''[[shimenawa]]'') and paper flags show that this tree has been sanctified as a ''kami''. The tree is alive with moss and ferns and other small plants growing on its ancient limbs. It is possible to enter the base of the tree, where there is a small altar for making offerings, and come out above ground level.<ref>{{Cite web|url = http://sacredjapan.com/Temple%2001/Nachi%20Taisha.htm|title = Nachi Taisha-Sacred Camphor Tree|access-date = October 9, 2014|website = Sacred Japan|last = Juno|first = Cate Kodo|archive-date = March 3, 2016|archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20160303212124/http://sacredjapan.com/Temple%2001/Nachi%20Taisha.htm|url-status = dead}}</ref>
Nachi-no-Hi Matsuri Fire Festival, performed on [[July 14]], is the major festival of Kumano Nachi Taisha. It is a fire festival in which six-meter-high portable shrines symbolically representing the purification of the waterfall with the fires from oversized torches is laboriously carried by men dressed in white.<ref>{{Cite web|url = http://www.tb-kumano.jp/en/world-heritage/kumano-nachi-taisha/|title = Kumano Nachi Taisha 熊野那智大社|access-date = October 9, 2014|website = Sacred Kumano|publisher = Tanabe City Kumano Tourism Bureau}}</ref>

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Nachikatsuura, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = {{Nihongo|Kumano Nachi Taisha|熊野那智大社}} | image = Kumano-Nachi-shrine002.JPG | caption = Kumano Nachi Taisha's ''[[Heiden (Shinto)|heiden]]'' | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|33|40|07|N|135|53|26|E|display=title,inline|region:JP_type:landmark_source:dewiki}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Fusumi no kami (Izanami no mikoto) and others<ref>Cali, Joseph. Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion. — Honolulu, 2013. — pp. 253-256.</ref> | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = [[Nachikatsuura]], [[Higashimuro District, Wakayama|Higashimuro District]], [[Wakayama Prefecture]], [[Japan]] | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }}
{{Nihongo|'''Kumano Nachi Taisha'''|熊野那智大社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] and part of the [[UNESCO]]-designated [[World Heritage]] [[Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range]] of [[Japan]]. The [[Kumano Kodō]] route connects it to other sites under the same classification, which are primarily located in [[Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan. The four sites on the route, classified as pilgrimage destinations and World Heritage Sites, are: 1) Nachi Taisha; 2) [[Kumano Hongū Taisha|Hongū Taisha]]; 3) [[Kumano Hayatama Taisha|Hayatama Taisha]]; 4) [[Mount Kōya|Koya-san]].
Kumano Nachi Taisha is also one of the three sacred [[Kumano Shrine|Kumano Sanzan]] shrines: # Kumano Nachi Taisha # [[Kumano Hongū Taisha]] # [[Kumano Hayatama Taisha]] This classification is based mostly in Japanese history, as pilgrims would travel to all three sites to complete their pilgrimage.
Kumano Nachi Taisha is an example of Buddhist and Shinto [[syncretism]] ([[Shinbutsu shūgō]]) nestled in the Kii Mountains, near Kii Katsuura, Japan. Cedar forests surround the site. The 133-meter [[Nachi Falls|Nachi Waterfall]], worshiped at the Hiryū Shrine near Kumano Nachi Taisha, is believed to be inhabited by a ''[[kami]]'' called Hiryū [[Gongen]].<ref name="nachi">{{cite web|url=http://www.kkr.mlit.go.jp/wakayama/rekishi/disco131.html|title=Hiryū Gongen|last=Kamizaka|first=Jirō|publisher=Ministry of Land, Infrastructure and Transport - Kinki Regional Development Bureau|language=ja|access-date=28 March 2010|archive-date=23 July 2011|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110723162553/http://www.kkr.mlit.go.jp/wakayama/rekishi/disco131.html|url-status=dead}}</ref> Also, there is a sacred tree at this site, the Sacred [[Camphor laurel|Camphor Tree]], located between the Nachi Shrine (''[[Heiden (Shinto)|heiden]]'') and [[Seiganto-ji|Seigantoji temple]]. It is 850 years old and is said to have been planted by [[Taira no Shigemori|Taira-no-Shigemori]] (1138–1179). The straw rope (''[[shimenawa]]'') and paper flags show that this tree has been sanctified as a ''kami''. The tree is alive with moss and ferns and other small plants growing on its ancient limbs. It is possible to enter the base of the tree, where there is a small altar for making offerings, and come out above ground level.<ref>{{Cite web|url = http://sacredjapan.com/Temple%2001/Nachi%20Taisha.htm|title = Nachi Taisha-Sacred Camphor Tree|access-date = October 9, 2014|website = Sacred Japan|last = Juno|first = Cate Kodo|archive-date = March 3, 2016|archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20160303212124/http://sacredjapan.com/Temple%2001/Nachi%20Taisha.htm|url-status = dead}}</ref>
Nachi-no-Hi Matsuri Fire Festival, performed on [[July 14]], is the major festival of Kumano Nachi Taisha. It is a fire festival in which six-meter-high portable shrines symbolically representing the purification of the waterfall with the fires from oversized torches is laboriously carried by men dressed in white.<ref>{{Cite web|url = http://www.tb-kumano.jp/en/world-heritage/kumano-nachi-taisha/|title = Kumano Nachi Taisha 熊野那智大社|access-date = October 9, 2014|website = Sacred Kumano|publisher = Tanabe City Kumano Tourism Bureau}}</ref>

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Nachikatsuura, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = {{Nihongo|Kumano Nachi Taisha|熊野那智大社}} | image = Kumano-Nachi-shrine002.JPG | caption = Kumano Nachi Taisha's ''[[Heiden (Shinto)|heiden]]'' | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|33|40|07|N|135|53|26|E|display=title,inline|region:JP_type:landmark_source:dewiki}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Fusumi no kami (Izanami no mikoto) and others<ref>Cali, Joseph. Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion. — Honolulu, 2013. — pp. 253-256.</ref> | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = [[Nachikatsuura]], [[Higashimuro District, Wakayama|Higashimuro District]], [[Wakayama Prefecture]], [[Japan]] | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }}
{{Nihongo|'''Kumano Nachi Taisha'''|熊野那智大社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] and part of the [[UNESCO]]-designated [[World Heritage]] [[Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range]] of [[Japan]]. The [[Kumano Kodō]] route connects it to other sites under the same classification, which are primarily located in [[Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan. The four sites on the route, classified as pilgrimage destinations and World Heritage Sites, are: 1) Nachi Taisha; 2) [[Kumano Hongū Taisha|Hongū Taisha]]; 3) [[Kumano Hayatama Taisha|Hayatama Taisha]]; 4) [[Mount Kōya|Koya-san]].
Kumano Nachi Taisha is also one of the three sacred [[Kumano Shrine|Kumano Sanzan]] shrines: # Kumano Nachi Taisha # [[Kumano Hongū Taisha]] # [[Kumano Hayatama Taisha]] This classification is based mostly in Japanese history, as pilgrims would travel to all three sites to complete their pilgrimage.
Kumano Nachi Taisha is an example of Buddhist and Shinto [[syncretism]] ([[Shinbutsu shūgō]]) nestled in the Kii Mountains, near Kii Katsuura, Japan. Cedar forests surround the site. The 133-meter [[Nachi Falls|Nachi Waterfall]], worshiped at the Hiryū Shrine near Kumano Nachi Taisha, is believed to be inhabited by a ''[[kami]]'' called Hiryū [[Gongen]].<ref name="nachi">{{cite web|url=http://www.kkr.mlit.go.jp/wakayama/rekishi/disco131.html|title=Hiryū Gongen|last=Kamizaka|first=Jirō|publisher=Ministry of Land, Infrastructure and Transport - Kinki Regional Development Bureau|language=ja|access-date=28 March 2010|archive-date=23 July 2011|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110723162553/http://www.kkr.mlit.go.jp/wakayama/rekishi/disco131.html|url-status=dead}}</ref> Also, there is a sacred tree at this site, the Sacred [[Camphor laurel|Camphor Tree]], located between the Nachi Shrine (''[[Heiden (Shinto)|heiden]]'') and [[Seiganto-ji|Seigantoji temple]]. It is 850 years old and is said to have been planted by [[Taira no Shigemori|Taira-no-Shigemori]] (1138–1179). The straw rope (''[[shimenawa]]'') and paper flags show that this tree has been sanctified as a ''kami''. The tree is alive with moss and ferns and other small plants growing on its ancient limbs. It is possible to enter the base of the tree, where there is a small altar for making offerings, and come out above ground level.<ref>{{Cite web|url = http://sacredjapan.com/Temple%2001/Nachi%20Taisha.htm|title = Nachi Taisha-Sacred Camphor Tree|access-date = October 9, 2014|website = Sacred Japan|last = Juno|first = Cate Kodo|archive-date = March 3, 2016|archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20160303212124/http://sacredjapan.com/Temple%2001/Nachi%20Taisha.htm|url-status = dead}}</ref>
Nachi-no-Hi Matsuri Fire Festival, performed on [[July 14]], is the major festival of Kumano Nachi Taisha. It is a fire festival in which six-meter-high portable shrines symbolically representing the purification of the waterfall with the fires from oversized torches is laboriously carried by men dressed in white.<ref>{{Cite web|url = http://www.tb-kumano.jp/en/world-heritage/kumano-nachi-taisha/|title = Kumano Nachi Taisha 熊野那智大社|access-date = October 9, 2014|website = Sacred Kumano|publisher = Tanabe City Kumano Tourism Bureau}}</ref>
==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]

</gallery>

{{Nihongo|'''Kumano Nachi Taisha'''|熊野那智大社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] and part of the [[UNESCO]]-designated [[World Heritage]] [[Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range]] of [[Japan]]. The [[Kumano Kodō]] route connects it to other sites under the same classification, which are primarily located in [[Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan. The four sites on the route, classified as pilgrimage destinations and World Heritage Sites, are: 1) Nachi Taisha; 2) [[Kumano Hongū Taisha|Hongū Taisha]]; 3) [[Kumano Hayatama Taisha|Hayatama Taisha]]; 4) [[Mount Kōya|Koya-san]].

Kumano Nachi Taisha is an example of Buddhist and Shinto [[syncretism]] ([[Shinbutsu shūgō]]) nestled in the Kii Mountains, near Kii Katsuura, Japan. Cedar forests surround the site. The 133-meter [[Nachi Falls|Nachi Waterfall]], worshiped at the Hiryū Shrine near Kumano Nachi Taisha, is believed to be inhabited by a ''[[kami]]'' called Hiryū [[Gongen]].<ref name="nachi">{{cite web|url=http://www.kkr.mlit.go.jp/wakayama/rekishi/disco131.html|title=Hiryū Gongen|last=Kamizaka|first=Jirō|publisher=Ministry of Land, Infrastructure and Transport - Kinki Regional Development Bureau|language=ja|access-date=28 March 2010|archive-date=23 July 2011|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110723162553/http://www.kkr.mlit.go.jp/wakayama/rekishi/disco131.html|url-status=dead}}</ref> Also, there is a sacred tree at this site, the Sacred [[Camphor laurel|Camphor Tree]], located between the Nachi Shrine (''[[Heiden (Shinto)|heiden]]'') and [[Seiganto-ji|Seigantoji temple]]. It is 850 years old and is said to have been planted by [[Taira no Shigemori|Taira-no-Shigemori]] (1138–1179). The straw rope (''[[shimenawa]]'') and paper flags show that this tree has been sanctified as a ''kami''. The tree is alive with moss and ferns and other small plants growing on its ancient limbs. It is possible to enter the base of the tree, where there is a small altar for making offerings, and come out above ground level.<ref>{{Cite web|url = http://sacredjapan.com/Temple%2001/Nachi%20Taisha.htm|title = Nachi Taisha-Sacred Camphor Tree|access-date = October 9, 2014|website = Sacred Japan|last = Juno|first = Cate Kodo|archive-date = March 3, 2016|archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20160303212124/http://sacredjapan.com/Temple%2001/Nachi%20Taisha.htm|url-status = dead}}</ref>

It is a fire festival in which six-meter-high portable shrines symbolically representing the purification of the waterfall with the fires from oversized torches is laboriously carried by men dressed in white.<ref>{{Cite web|url = http://www.tb-kumano.jp/en/world-heritage/kumano-nachi-taisha/|title = Kumano Nachi Taisha 熊野那智大社|access-date = October 9, 2014|website = Sacred Kumano|publisher = Tanabe City Kumano Tourism Bureau}}</ref>

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Nachikatsuura, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = {{Nihongo|Kumano Nachi Taisha|熊野那智大社}} | image = Kumano-Nachi-shrine002.JPG | caption = Kumano Nachi Taisha's ''[[Heiden (Shinto)|heiden]]'' | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|33|40|07|N|135|53|26|E|display=title,inline|region:JP_type:landmark_source:dewiki}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Fusumi no kami (Izanami no mikoto) and others<ref>Cali, Joseph. Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion. — Honolulu, 2013. — pp. 253-256.</ref> | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = [[Nachikatsuura]], [[Higashimuro District, Wakayama|Higashimuro District]], [[Wakayama Prefecture]], [[Japan]] | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }}
{{Nihongo|'''Kumano Nachi Taisha'''|熊野那智大社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] and part of the [[UNESCO]]-designated [[World Heritage]] [[Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range]] of [[Japan]]. The [[Kumano Kodō]] route connects it to other sites under the same classification, which are primarily located in [[Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan. The four sites on the route, classified as pilgrimage destinations and World Heritage Sites, are: 1) Nachi Taisha; 2) [[Kumano Hongū Taisha|Hongū Taisha]]; 3) [[Kumano Hayatama Taisha|Hayatama Taisha]]; 4) [[Mount Kōya|Koya-san]].
Kumano Nachi Taisha is also one of the three sacred [[Kumano Shrine|Kumano Sanzan]] shrines: # Kumano Nachi Taisha # [[Kumano Hongū Taisha]] # [[Kumano Hayatama Taisha]] This classification is based mostly in Japanese history, as pilgrims would travel to all three sites to complete their pilgrimage.
Kumano Nachi Taisha is an example of Buddhist and Shinto [[syncretism]] ([[Shinbutsu shūgō]]) nestled in the Kii Mountains, near Kii Katsuura, Japan. Cedar forests surround the site. The 133-meter [[Nachi Falls|Nachi Waterfall]], worshiped at the Hiryū Shrine near Kumano Nachi Taisha, is believed to be inhabited by a ''[[kami]]'' called Hiryū [[Gongen]].<ref name="nachi">{{cite web|url=http://www.kkr.mlit.go.jp/wakayama/rekishi/disco131.html|title=Hiryū Gongen|last=Kamizaka|first=Jirō|publisher=Ministry of Land, Infrastructure and Transport - Kinki Regional Development Bureau|language=ja|access-date=28 March 2010|archive-date=23 July 2011|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110723162553/http://www.kkr.mlit.go.jp/wakayama/rekishi/disco131.html|url-status=dead}}</ref> Also, there is a sacred tree at this site, the Sacred [[Camphor laurel|Camphor Tree]], located between the Nachi Shrine (''[[Heiden (Shinto)|heiden]]'') and [[Seiganto-ji|Seigantoji temple]]. It is 850 years old and is said to have been planted by [[Taira no Shigemori|Taira-no-Shigemori]] (1138–1179). The straw rope (''[[shimenawa]]'') and paper flags show that this tree has been sanctified as a ''kami''. The tree is alive with moss and ferns and other small plants growing on its ancient limbs. It is possible to enter the base of the tree, where there is a small altar for making offerings, and come out above ground level.<ref>{{Cite web|url = http://sacredjapan.com/Temple%2001/Nachi%20Taisha.htm|title = Nachi Taisha-Sacred Camphor Tree|access-date = October 9, 2014|website = Sacred Japan|last = Juno|first = Cate Kodo|archive-date = March 3, 2016|archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20160303212124/http://sacredjapan.com/Temple%2001/Nachi%20Taisha.htm|url-status = dead}}</ref>
Nachi-no-Hi Matsuri Fire Festival, performed on [[July 14]], is the major festival of Kumano Nachi Taisha. It is a fire festival in which six-meter-high portable shrines symbolically representing the purification of the waterfall with the fires from oversized torches is laboriously carried by men dressed in white.<ref>{{Cite web|url = http://www.tb-kumano.jp/en/world-heritage/kumano-nachi-taisha/|title = Kumano Nachi Taisha 熊野那智大社|access-date = October 9, 2014|website = Sacred Kumano|publisher = Tanabe City Kumano Tourism Bureau}}</ref>

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Nachikatsuura, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = {{Nihongo|Kumano Nachi Taisha|熊野那智大社}} | image = Kumano-Nachi-shrine002.JPG | caption = Kumano Nachi Taisha's ''[[Heiden (Shinto)|heiden]]'' | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|33|40|07|N|135|53|26|E|display=title,inline|region:JP_type:landmark_source:dewiki}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Fusumi no kami (Izanami no mikoto) and others<ref>Cali, Joseph. Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion. — Honolulu, 2013. — pp. 253-256.</ref> | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = [[Nachikatsuura]], [[Higashimuro District, Wakayama|Higashimuro District]], [[Wakayama Prefecture]], [[Japan]] | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }}
{{Nihongo|'''Kumano Nachi Taisha'''|熊野那智大社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] and part of the [[UNESCO]]-designated [[World Heritage]] [[Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range]] of [[Japan]]. The [[Kumano Kodō]] route connects it to other sites under the same classification, which are primarily located in [[Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan. The four sites on the route, classified as pilgrimage destinations and World Heritage Sites, are: 1) Nachi Taisha; 2) [[Kumano Hongū Taisha|Hongū Taisha]]; 3) [[Kumano Hayatama Taisha|Hayatama Taisha]]; 4) [[Mount Kōya|Koya-san]].
Kumano Nachi Taisha is also one of the three sacred [[Kumano Shrine|Kumano Sanzan]] shrines: # Kumano Nachi Taisha # [[Kumano Hongū Taisha]] # [[Kumano Hayatama Taisha]] This classification is based mostly in Japanese history, as pilgrims would travel to all three sites to complete their pilgrimage.
Kumano Nachi Taisha is an example of Buddhist and Shinto [[syncretism]] ([[Shinbutsu shūgō]]) nestled in the Kii Mountains, near Kii Katsuura, Japan. Cedar forests surround the site. The 133-meter [[Nachi Falls|Nachi Waterfall]], worshiped at the Hiryū Shrine near Kumano Nachi Taisha, is believed to be inhabited by a ''[[kami]]'' called Hiryū [[Gongen]].<ref name="nachi">{{cite web|url=http://www.kkr.mlit.go.jp/wakayama/rekishi/disco131.html|title=Hiryū Gongen|last=Kamizaka|first=Jirō|publisher=Ministry of Land, Infrastructure and Transport - Kinki Regional Development Bureau|language=ja|access-date=28 March 2010|archive-date=23 July 2011|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110723162553/http://www.kkr.mlit.go.jp/wakayama/rekishi/disco131.html|url-status=dead}}</ref> Also, there is a sacred tree at this site, the Sacred [[Camphor laurel|Camphor Tree]], located between the Nachi Shrine (''[[Heiden (Shinto)|heiden]]'') and [[Seiganto-ji|Seigantoji temple]]. It is 850 years old and is said to have been planted by [[Taira no Shigemori|Taira-no-Shigemori]] (1138–1179). The straw rope (''[[shimenawa]]'') and paper flags show that this tree has been sanctified as a ''kami''. The tree is alive with moss and ferns and other small plants growing on its ancient limbs. It is possible to enter the base of the tree, where there is a small altar for making offerings, and come out above ground level.<ref>{{Cite web|url = http://sacredjapan.com/Temple%2001/Nachi%20Taisha.htm|title = Nachi Taisha-Sacred Camphor Tree|access-date = October 9, 2014|website = Sacred Japan|last = Juno|first = Cate Kodo|archive-date = March 3, 2016|archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20160303212124/http://sacredjapan.com/Temple%2001/Nachi%20Taisha.htm|url-status = dead}}</ref>
Nachi-no-Hi Matsuri Fire Festival, performed on [[July 14]], is the major festival of Kumano Nachi Taisha. It is a fire festival in which six-meter-high portable shrines symbolically representing the purification of the waterfall with the fires from oversized torches is laboriously carried by men dressed in white.<ref>{{Cite web|url = http://www.tb-kumano.jp/en/world-heritage/kumano-nachi-taisha/|title = Kumano Nachi Taisha 熊野那智大社|access-date = October 9, 2014|website = Sacred Kumano|publisher = Tanabe City Kumano Tourism Bureau}}</ref>
==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Nachikatsuura, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = {{Nihongo|Kumano Nachi Taisha|熊野那智大社}} | image = Kumano-Nachi-shrine002.JPG | caption = Kumano Nachi Taisha's ''[[Heiden (Shinto)|heiden]]'' | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|33|40|07|N|135|53|26|E|display=title,inline|region:JP_type:landmark_source:dewiki}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Fusumi no kami (Izanami no mikoto) and others<ref>Cali, Joseph. Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion. — Honolulu, 2013. — pp. 253-256.</ref> | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = [[Nachikatsuura]], [[Higashimuro District, Wakayama|Higashimuro District]], [[Wakayama Prefecture]], [[Japan]] | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }}
{{Nihongo|'''Kumano Nachi Taisha'''|熊野那智大社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] and part of the [[UNESCO]]-designated [[World Heritage]] [[Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range]] of [[Japan]]. The [[Kumano Kodō]] route connects it to other sites under the same classification, which are primarily located in [[Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan. The four sites on the route, classified as pilgrimage destinations and World Heritage Sites, are: 1) Nachi Taisha; 2) [[Kumano Hongū Taisha|Hongū Taisha]]; 3) [[Kumano Hayatama Taisha|Hayatama Taisha]]; 4) [[Mount Kōya|Koya-san]].
Kumano Nachi Taisha is also one of the three sacred [[Kumano Shrine|Kumano Sanzan]] shrines: # Kumano Nachi Taisha # [[Kumano Hongū Taisha]] # [[Kumano Hayatama Taisha]] This classification is based mostly in Japanese history, as pilgrims would travel to all three sites to complete their pilgrimage.
Kumano Nachi Taisha is an example of Buddhist and Shinto [[syncretism]] ([[Shinbutsu shūgō]]) nestled in the Kii Mountains, near Kii Katsuura, Japan. Cedar forests surround the site. The 133-meter [[Nachi Falls|Nachi Waterfall]], worshiped at the Hiryū Shrine near Kumano Nachi Taisha, is believed to be inhabited by a ''[[kami]]'' called Hiryū [[Gongen]].<ref name="nachi">{{cite web|url=http://www.kkr.mlit.go.jp/wakayama/rekishi/disco131.html|title=Hiryū Gongen|last=Kamizaka|first=Jirō|publisher=Ministry of Land, Infrastructure and Transport - Kinki Regional Development Bureau|language=ja|access-date=28 March 2010|archive-date=23 July 2011|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110723162553/http://www.kkr.mlit.go.jp/wakayama/rekishi/disco131.html|url-status=dead}}</ref> Also, there is a sacred tree at this site, the Sacred [[Camphor laurel|Camphor Tree]], located between the Nachi Shrine (''[[Heiden (Shinto)|heiden]]'') and [[Seiganto-ji|Seigantoji temple]]. It is 850 years old and is said to have been planted by [[Taira no Shigemori|Taira-no-Shigemori]] (1138–1179). The straw rope (''[[shimenawa]]'') and paper flags show that this tree has been sanctified as a ''kami''. The tree is alive with moss and ferns and other small plants growing on its ancient limbs. It is possible to enter the base of the tree, where there is a small altar for making offerings, and come out above ground level.<ref>{{Cite web|url = http://sacredjapan.com/Temple%2001/Nachi%20Taisha.htm|title = Nachi Taisha-Sacred Camphor Tree|access-date = October 9, 2014|website = Sacred Japan|last = Juno|first = Cate Kodo|archive-date = March 3, 2016|archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20160303212124/http://sacredjapan.com/Temple%2001/Nachi%20Taisha.htm|url-status = dead}}</ref>
Nachi-no-Hi Matsuri Fire Festival, performed on [[July 14]], is the major festival of Kumano Nachi Taisha. It is a fire festival in which six-meter-high portable shrines symbolically representing the purification of the waterfall with the fires from oversized torches is laboriously carried by men dressed in white.<ref>{{Cite web|url = http://www.tb-kumano.jp/en/world-heritage/kumano-nachi-taisha/|title = Kumano Nachi Taisha 熊野那智大社|access-date = October 9, 2014|website = Sacred Kumano|publisher = Tanabe City Kumano Tourism Bureau}}</ref>

{{Nihongo|'''Kumano Nachi Taisha'''|熊野那智大社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] and part of the [[UNESCO]]-designated [[World Heritage]] [[Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range]] of [[Japan]]. The [[Kumano Kodō]] route connects it to other sites under the same classification, which are primarily located in [[Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan. The four sites on the route, classified as pilgrimage destinations and World Heritage Sites, are: 1) Nachi Taisha; 2) [[Kumano Hongū Taisha|Hongū Taisha]]; 3) [[Kumano Hayatama Taisha|Hayatama Taisha]]; 4) [[Mount Kōya|Koya-san]].
Kumano Nachi Taisha is also one of the three sacred [[Kumano Shrine|Kumano Sanzan]] shrines: # Kumano Nachi Taisha # [[Kumano Hongū Taisha]] # [[Kumano Hayatama Taisha]] This classification is based mostly in Japanese history, as pilgrims would travel to all three sites to complete their pilgrimage.
Kumano Nachi Taisha is an example of Buddhist and Shinto [[syncretism]] ([[Shinbutsu shūgō]]) nestled in the Kii Mountains, near Kii Katsuura, Japan. Cedar forests surround the site. The 133-meter [[Nachi Falls|Nachi Waterfall]], worshiped at the Hiryū Shrine near Kumano Nachi Taisha, is believed to be inhabited by a ''[[kami]]'' called Hiryū [[Gongen]].<ref name="nachi">{{cite web|url=http://www.kkr.mlit.go.jp/wakayama/rekishi/disco131.html|title=Hiryū Gongen|last=Kamizaka|first=Jirō|publisher=Ministry of Land, Infrastructure and Transport - Kinki Regional Development Bureau|language=ja|access-date=28 March 2010|archive-date=23 July 2011|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110723162553/http://www.kkr.mlit.go.jp/wakayama/rekishi/disco131.html|url-status=dead}}</ref> Also, there is a sacred tree at this site, the Sacred [[Camphor laurel|Camphor Tree]], located between the Nachi Shrine (''[[Heiden (Shinto)|heiden]]'') and [[Seiganto-ji|Seigantoji temple]]. It is 850 years old and is said to have been planted by [[Taira no Shigemori|Taira-no-Shigemori]] (1138–1179). The straw rope (''[[shimenawa]]'') and paper flags show that this tree has been sanctified as a ''kami''. The tree is alive with moss and ferns and other small plants growing on its ancient limbs. It is possible to enter the base of the tree, where there is a small altar for making offerings, and come out above ground level.<ref>{{Cite web|url = http://sacredjapan.com/Temple%2001/Nachi%20Taisha.htm|title = Nachi Taisha-Sacred Camphor Tree|access-date = October 9, 2014|website = Sacred Japan|last = Juno|first = Cate Kodo|archive-date = March 3, 2016|archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20160303212124/http://sacredjapan.com/Temple%2001/Nachi%20Taisha.htm|url-status = dead}}</ref>
Nachi-no-Hi Matsuri Fire Festival, performed on [[July 14]], is the major festival of Kumano Nachi Taisha. It is a fire festival in which six-meter-high portable shrines symbolically representing the purification of the waterfall with the fires from oversized torches is laboriously carried by men dressed in white.<ref>{{Cite web|url = http://www.tb-kumano.jp/en/world-heritage/kumano-nachi-taisha/|title = Kumano Nachi Taisha 熊野那智大社|access-date = October 9, 2014|website = Sacred Kumano|publisher = Tanabe City Kumano Tourism Bureau}}</ref>

Kumano Nachi Taisha is an example of Buddhist and Shinto [[syncretism]] ([[Shinbutsu shūgō]]) nestled in the Kii Mountains, near Kii Katsuura, Japan. Cedar forests surround the site. The 133-meter [[Nachi Falls|Nachi Waterfall]], worshiped at the Hiryū Shrine near Kumano Nachi Taisha, is believed to be inhabited by a ''[[kami]]'' called Hiryū [[Gongen]].<ref name="nachi">{{cite web|url=http://www.kkr.mlit.go.jp/wakayama/rekishi/disco131.html|title=Hiryū Gongen|last=Kamizaka|first=Jirō|publisher=Ministry of Land, Infrastructure and Transport - Kinki Regional Development Bureau|language=ja|access-date=28 March 2010|archive-date=23 July 2011|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110723162553/http://www.kkr.mlit.go.jp/wakayama/rekishi/disco131.html|url-status=dead}}</ref> Also, there is a sacred tree at this site, the Sacred [[Camphor laurel|Camphor Tree]], located between the Nachi Shrine (''[[Heiden (Shinto)|heiden]]'') and [[Seiganto-ji|Seigantoji temple]]. It is 850 years old and is said to have been planted by [[Taira no Shigemori|Taira-no-Shigemori]] (1138–1179). The straw rope (''[[shimenawa]]'') and paper flags show that this tree has been sanctified as a ''kami''. The tree is alive with moss and ferns and other small plants growing on its ancient limbs. It is possible to enter the base of the tree, where there is a small altar for making offerings, and come out above ground level.<ref>{{Cite web|url = http://sacredjapan.com/Temple%2001/Nachi%20Taisha.htm|title = Nachi Taisha-Sacred Camphor Tree|access-date = October 9, 2014|website = Sacred Japan|last = Juno|first = Cate Kodo|archive-date = March 3, 2016|archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20160303212124/http://sacredjapan.com/Temple%2001/Nachi%20Taisha.htm|url-status = dead}}</ref>
Nachi-no-Hi Matsuri Fire Festival, performed on [[July 14]], is the major festival of Kumano Nachi Taisha. It is a fire festival in which six-meter-high portable shrines symbolically representing the purification of the waterfall with the fires from oversized torches is laboriously carried by men dressed in white.<ref>{{Cite web|url = http://www.tb-kumano.jp/en/world-heritage/kumano-nachi-taisha/|title = Kumano Nachi Taisha 熊野那智大社|access-date = October 9, 2014|website = Sacred Kumano|publisher = Tanabe City Kumano Tourism Bureau}}</ref>

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Nachikatsuura, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = {{Nihongo|Kumano Nachi Taisha|熊野那智大社}} | image = Kumano-Nachi-shrine002.JPG | caption = Kumano Nachi Taisha's ''[[Heiden (Shinto)|heiden]]'' | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|33|40|07|N|135|53|26|E|display=title,inline|region:JP_type:landmark_source:dewiki}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Fusumi no kami (Izanami no mikoto) and others<ref>Cali, Joseph. Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion. — Honolulu, 2013. — pp. 253-256.</ref> | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = [[Nachikatsuura]], [[Higashimuro District, Wakayama|Higashimuro District]], [[Wakayama Prefecture]], [[Japan]] | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }}
{{Nihongo|'''Kumano Nachi Taisha'''|熊野那智大社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] and part of the [[UNESCO]]-designated [[World Heritage]] [[Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range]] of [[Japan]]. The [[Kumano Kodō]] route connects it to other sites under the same classification, which are primarily located in [[Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan. The four sites on the route, classified as pilgrimage destinations and World Heritage Sites, are: 1) Nachi Taisha; 2) [[Kumano Hongū Taisha|Hongū Taisha]]; 3) [[Kumano Hayatama Taisha|Hayatama Taisha]]; 4) [[Mount Kōya|Koya-san]].
Kumano Nachi Taisha is also one of the three sacred [[Kumano Shrine|Kumano Sanzan]] shrines: # Kumano Nachi Taisha # [[Kumano Hongū Taisha]] # [[Kumano Hayatama Taisha]] This classification is based mostly in Japanese history, as pilgrims would travel to all three sites to complete their pilgrimage.
Kumano Nachi Taisha is an example of Buddhist and Shinto [[syncretism]] ([[Shinbutsu shūgō]]) nestled in the Kii Mountains, near Kii Katsuura, Japan. Cedar forests surround the site. The 133-meter [[Nachi Falls|Nachi Waterfall]], worshiped at the Hiryū Shrine near Kumano Nachi Taisha, is believed to be inhabited by a ''[[kami]]'' called Hiryū [[Gongen]].<ref name="nachi">{{cite web|url=http://www.kkr.mlit.go.jp/wakayama/rekishi/disco131.html|title=Hiryū Gongen|last=Kamizaka|first=Jirō|publisher=Ministry of Land, Infrastructure and Transport - Kinki Regional Development Bureau|language=ja|access-date=28 March 2010|archive-date=23 July 2011|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110723162553/http://www.kkr.mlit.go.jp/wakayama/rekishi/disco131.html|url-status=dead}}</ref> Also, there is a sacred tree at this site, the Sacred [[Camphor laurel|Camphor Tree]], located between the Nachi Shrine (''[[Heiden (Shinto)|heiden]]'') and [[Seiganto-ji|Seigantoji temple]]. It is 850 years old and is said to have been planted by [[Taira no Shigemori|Taira-no-Shigemori]] (1138–1179). The straw rope (''[[shimenawa]]'') and paper flags show that this tree has been sanctified as a ''kami''. The tree is alive with moss and ferns and other small plants growing on its ancient limbs. It is possible to enter the base of the tree, where there is a small altar for making offerings, and come out above ground level.<ref>{{Cite web|url = http://sacredjapan.com/Temple%2001/Nachi%20Taisha.htm|title = Nachi Taisha-Sacred Camphor Tree|access-date = October 9, 2014|website = Sacred Japan|last = Juno|first = Cate Kodo|archive-date = March 3, 2016|archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20160303212124/http://sacredjapan.com/Temple%2001/Nachi%20Taisha.htm|url-status = dead}}</ref>
Nachi-no-Hi Matsuri Fire Festival, performed on [[July 14]], is the major festival of Kumano Nachi Taisha. It is a fire festival in which six-meter-high portable shrines symbolically representing the purification of the waterfall with the fires from oversized torches is laboriously carried by men dressed in white.<ref>{{Cite web|url = http://www.tb-kumano.jp/en/world-heritage/kumano-nachi-taisha/|title = Kumano Nachi Taisha 熊野那智大社|access-date = October 9, 2014|website = Sacred Kumano|publisher = Tanabe City Kumano Tourism Bureau}}</ref>

revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Nachikatsuura, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = {{Nihongo|Kumano Nachi Taisha|熊野那智大社}} | image = Kumano-Nachi-shrine002.JPG | caption = Kumano Nachi Taisha's ''[[Heiden (Shinto)|heiden]]'' | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|33|40|07|N|135|53|26|E|display=title,inline|region:JP_type:landmark_source:dewiki}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Fusumi no kami (Izanami no mikoto) and others<ref>Cali, Joseph. Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion. — Honolulu, 2013. — pp. 253-256.</ref> | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = [[Nachikatsuura]], [[Higashimuro District, Wakayama|Higashimuro District]], [[Wakayama Prefecture]], [[Japan]] | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }}
{{Nihongo|'''Kumano Nachi Taisha'''|熊野那智大社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] and part of the [[UNESCO]]-designated [[World Heritage]] [[Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range]] of [[Japan]]. The [[Kumano Kodō]] route connects it to other sites under the same classification, which are primarily located in [[Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan. The four sites on the route, classified as pilgrimage destinations and World Heritage Sites, are: 1) Nachi Taisha; 2) [[Kumano Hongū Taisha|Hongū Taisha]]; 3) [[Kumano Hayatama Taisha|Hayatama Taisha]]; 4) [[Mount Kōya|Koya-san]].
Kumano Nachi Taisha is also one of the three sacred [[Kumano Shrine|Kumano Sanzan]] shrines: # Kumano Nachi Taisha # [[Kumano Hongū Taisha]] # [[Kumano Hayatama Taisha]] This classification is based mostly in Japanese history, as pilgrims would travel to all three sites to complete their pilgrimage.
Kumano Nachi Taisha is an example of Buddhist and Shinto [[syncretism]] ([[Shinbutsu shūgō]]) nestled in the Kii Mountains, near Kii Katsuura, Japan. Cedar forests surround the site. The 133-meter [[Nachi Falls|Nachi Waterfall]], worshiped at the Hiryū Shrine near Kumano Nachi Taisha, is believed to be inhabited by a ''[[kami]]'' called Hiryū [[Gongen]].<ref name="nachi">{{cite web|url=http://www.kkr.mlit.go.jp/wakayama/rekishi/disco131.html|title=Hiryū Gongen|last=Kamizaka|first=Jirō|publisher=Ministry of Land, Infrastructure and Transport - Kinki Regional Development Bureau|language=ja|access-date=28 March 2010|archive-date=23 July 2011|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110723162553/http://www.kkr.mlit.go.jp/wakayama/rekishi/disco131.html|url-status=dead}}</ref> Also, there is a sacred tree at this site, the Sacred [[Camphor laurel|Camphor Tree]], located between the Nachi Shrine (''[[Heiden (Shinto)|heiden]]'') and [[Seiganto-ji|Seigantoji temple]]. It is 850 years old and is said to have been planted by [[Taira no Shigemori|Taira-no-Shigemori]] (1138–1179). The straw rope (''[[shimenawa]]'') and paper flags show that this tree has been sanctified as a ''kami''. The tree is alive with moss and ferns and other small plants growing on its ancient limbs. It is possible to enter the base of the tree, where there is a small altar for making offerings, and come out above ground level.<ref>{{Cite web|url = http://sacredjapan.com/Temple%2001/Nachi%20Taisha.htm|title = Nachi Taisha-Sacred Camphor Tree|access-date = October 9, 2014|website = Sacred Japan|last = Juno|first = Cate Kodo|archive-date = March 3, 2016|archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20160303212124/http://sacredjapan.com/Temple%2001/Nachi%20Taisha.htm|url-status = dead}}</ref>
Nachi-no-Hi Matsuri Fire Festival, performed on [[July 14]], is the major festival of Kumano Nachi Taisha. It is a fire festival in which six-meter-high portable shrines symbolically representing the purification of the waterfall with the fires from oversized torches is laboriously carried by men dressed in white.<ref>{{Cite web|url = http://www.tb-kumano.jp/en/world-heritage/kumano-nachi-taisha/|title = Kumano Nachi Taisha 熊野那智大社|access-date = October 9, 2014|website = Sacred Kumano|publisher = Tanabe City Kumano Tourism Bureau}}</ref>
==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Nachikatsuura, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = {{Nihongo|Kumano Nachi Taisha|熊野那智大社}} | image = Kumano-Nachi-shrine002.JPG | caption = Kumano Nachi Taisha's ''[[Heiden (Shinto)|heiden]]'' | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|33|40|07|N|135|53|26|E|display=title,inline|region:JP_type:landmark_source:dewiki}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Fusumi no kami (Izanami no mikoto) and others<ref>Cali, Joseph. Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion. — Honolulu, 2013. — pp. 253-256.</ref> | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = [[Nachikatsuura]], [[Higashimuro District, Wakayama|Higashimuro District]], [[Wakayama Prefecture]], [[Japan]] | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }}
{{Nihongo|'''Kumano Nachi Taisha'''|熊野那智大社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] and part of the [[UNESCO]]-designated [[World Heritage]] [[Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range]] of [[Japan]]. The [[Kumano Kodō]] route connects it to other sites under the same classification, which are primarily located in [[Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan. The four sites on the route, classified as pilgrimage destinations and World Heritage Sites, are: 1) Nachi Taisha; 2) [[Kumano Hongū Taisha|Hongū Taisha]]; 3) [[Kumano Hayatama Taisha|Hayatama Taisha]]; 4) [[Mount Kōya|Koya-san]].
Kumano Nachi Taisha is also one of the three sacred [[Kumano Shrine|Kumano Sanzan]] shrines: # Kumano Nachi Taisha # [[Kumano Hongū Taisha]] # [[Kumano Hayatama Taisha]] This classification is based mostly in Japanese history, as pilgrims would travel to all three sites to complete their pilgrimage.
Kumano Nachi Taisha is an example of Buddhist and Shinto [[syncretism]] ([[Shinbutsu shūgō]]) nestled in the Kii Mountains, near Kii Katsuura, Japan. Cedar forests surround the site. The 133-meter [[Nachi Falls|Nachi Waterfall]], worshiped at the Hiryū Shrine near Kumano Nachi Taisha, is believed to be inhabited by a ''[[kami]]'' called Hiryū [[Gongen]].<ref name="nachi">{{cite web|url=http://www.kkr.mlit.go.jp/wakayama/rekishi/disco131.html|title=Hiryū Gongen|last=Kamizaka|first=Jirō|publisher=Ministry of Land, Infrastructure and Transport - Kinki Regional Development Bureau|language=ja|access-date=28 March 2010|archive-date=23 July 2011|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110723162553/http://www.kkr.mlit.go.jp/wakayama/rekishi/disco131.html|url-status=dead}}</ref> Also, there is a sacred tree at this site, the Sacred [[Camphor laurel|Camphor Tree]], located between the Nachi Shrine (''[[Heiden (Shinto)|heiden]]'') and [[Seiganto-ji|Seigantoji temple]]. It is 850 years old and is said to have been planted by [[Taira no Shigemori|Taira-no-Shigemori]] (1138–1179). The straw rope (''[[shimenawa]]'') and paper flags show that this tree has been sanctified as a ''kami''. The tree is alive with moss and ferns and other small plants growing on its ancient limbs. It is possible to enter the base of the tree, where there is a small altar for making offerings, and come out above ground level.<ref>{{Cite web|url = http://sacredjapan.com/Temple%2001/Nachi%20Taisha.htm|title = Nachi Taisha-Sacred Camphor Tree|access-date = October 9, 2014|website = Sacred Japan|last = Juno|first = Cate Kodo|archive-date = March 3, 2016|archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20160303212124/http://sacredjapan.com/Temple%2001/Nachi%20Taisha.htm|url-status = dead}}</ref>
Nachi-no-Hi Matsuri Fire Festival, performed on [[July 14]], is the major festival of Kumano Nachi Taisha. It is a fire festival in which six-meter-high portable shrines symbolically representing the purification of the waterfall with the fires from oversized torches is laboriously carried by men dressed in white.<ref>{{Cite web|url = http://www.tb-kumano.jp/en/world-heritage/kumano-nachi-taisha/|title = Kumano Nachi Taisha 熊野那智大社|access-date = October 9, 2014|website = Sacred Kumano|publisher = Tanabe City Kumano Tourism Bureau}}</ref>

revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Nachikatsuura, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = {{Nihongo|Kumano Nachi Taisha|熊野那智大社}} | image = Kumano-Nachi-shrine002.JPG | caption = Kumano Nachi Taisha's ''[[Heiden (Shinto)|heiden]]'' | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|33|40|07|N|135|53|26|E|display=title,inline|region:JP_type:landmark_source:dewiki}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Fusumi no kami (Izanami no mikoto) and others<ref>Cali, Joseph. Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion. — Honolulu, 2013. — pp. 253-256.</ref> | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = [[:en:Nachikatsuura|Nachikatsuura]], [[Higashimuro District, Wakayama|Higashimuro District]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }}
{{Nihongo|'''Kumano Nachi Taisha'''|熊野那智大社}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] and part of the [[:en:UNESCO|UNESCO]]-designated [[:en:World Heritage|World Heritage]] [[:en:Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range|Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The [[Kumano Kodō]] route connects it to other sites under the same classification, which are primarily located in [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan. The four sites on the route, classified as pilgrimage destinations and World Heritage Sites, are: 1) Nachi Taisha; 2) [[Kumano Hongū Taisha|Hongū Taisha]]; 3) [[Kumano Hayatama Taisha|Hayatama Taisha]]; 4) [[:en:Mount Kōya|Koya-san]].
Kumano Nachi Taisha is also one of the three sacred [[Kumano shrine|Kumano Sanzan]] shrines: # Kumano Nachi Taisha # [[Kumano Hongū Taisha]] # [[Kumano Hayatama Taisha]] This classification is based mostly in Japanese history, as pilgrims would travel to all three sites to complete their pilgrimage.
Kumano Nachi Taisha is an example of Buddhist and Shinto [[:en:Syncretism|Syncretism]] ([[Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu shūgō]]) nestled in the Kii Mountains, near Kii Katsuura, Japan. Cedar forests surround the site. The 133-meter [[Nachi Falls|Nachi Waterfall]], worshiped at the Hiryū Shrine near Kumano Nachi Taisha, is believed to be inhabited by a ''[[kami]]'' called Hiryū [[Gongen]].<ref name="nachi">{{cite web|url=http://www.kkr.mlit.go.jp/wakayama/rekishi/disco131.html|title=Hiryū Gongen|last=Kamizaka|first=Jirō|publisher=Ministry of Land, Infrastructure and Transport - Kinki Regional Development Bureau|language=ja|access-date=28 March 2010|archive-date=23 July 2011|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110723162553/http://www.kkr.mlit.go.jp/wakayama/rekishi/disco131.html|url-status=dead}}</ref> Also, there is a sacred tree at this site, the Sacred [[:en:Camphor laurel|Camphor Tree]], located between the Nachi Shrine (''[[Heiden (Shinto)|heiden]]'') and [[Seiganto-ji|Seigantoji temple]]. It is 850 years old and is said to have been planted by [[:en:Taira no Shigemori|Taira-no-Shigemori]] (1138–1179). The straw rope (''[[:en:Shimenawa|Shimenawa]]'') and paper flags show that this tree has been sanctified as a ''kami''. The tree is alive with moss and ferns and other small plants growing on its ancient limbs. It is possible to enter the base of the tree, where there is a small altar for making offerings, and come out above ground level.<ref>{{Cite web|url = http://sacredjapan.com/Temple%2001/Nachi%20Taisha.htm|title = Nachi Taisha-Sacred Camphor Tree|access-date = October 9, 2014|website = Sacred Japan|last = Juno|first = Cate Kodo|archive-date = March 3, 2016|archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20160303212124/http://sacredjapan.com/Temple%2001/Nachi%20Taisha.htm|url-status = dead}}</ref>
Nachi-no-Hi Matsuri Fire Festival, performed on [[:en:July 14|July 14]], is the major festival of Kumano Nachi Taisha. It is a fire festival in which six-meter-high portable shrines symbolically representing the purification of the waterfall with the fires from oversized torches is laboriously carried by men dressed in white.<ref>{{Cite web|url = http://www.tb-kumano.jp/en/world-heritage/kumano-nachi-taisha/|title = Kumano Nachi Taisha 熊野那智大社|access-date = October 9, 2014|website = Sacred Kumano|publisher = Tanabe City Kumano Tourism Bureau}}</ref>
==See also== * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] * [[:en:Twenty-Two Shrines|Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]
==References== {{Reflist}}
==External links== *[http://www.tb-kumano.jp/en/index.html#2 Tanabe City Kumano Tourism Bureau] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110925191301/http://www.tb-kumano.jp/en/index.html#2 |date=2011-09-25 }} *[http://www.kumadoco.net/kodo_eng/about/index.html#no2 Description of the sites] **[https://web.archive.org/web/20110610222210/http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/sekaiisan/english/e-kumano_si.html Wakayama Prefecture webpage about Kumano Nachi Taisha] *[http://www.nachikan.jp/en/ Nachi-Katsuura Town Tourism Association]
==Gallery== <gallery perrow="4" widths="170px" heights="130px"> Nachi-san Pagoda (Japan 2007 061).jpg|Nachi-san Pagoda Nachi Taisha's main temple (Japan 2007 082).jpg|Nachi Taisha's main temple Nachi waterfall (Japan 2007 047).jpg|Nachi waterfall Sanjūdō pagoda (Japan 2007 069).jpg|Sanjūdō pagoda, Nachi Taisha
</gallery>

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q710359]] Wikidata name: Kumano Nachi Taisha [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:43, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Nachikatsuura, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = {{Nihongo|Kumano Nachi Taisha|熊野那智大社}} | image = Kumano-Nachi-shrine002.JPG | caption = Kumano Nachi Taisha's ''[[Heiden (Shinto)|heiden]]'' | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|33|40|07|N|135|53|26|E|display=title,inline|region:JP_type:landmark_source:dewiki}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Fusumi no kami (Izanami no mikoto) and others<ref>Cali, Joseph. Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion. — Honolulu, 2013. — pp. 253-256.</ref> | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = [[:en:Nachikatsuura|Nachikatsuura]], [[Higashimuro District, Wakayama|Higashimuro District]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }}
{{Nihongo|'''Kumano Nachi Taisha'''|熊野那智大社}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] and part of the [[:en:UNESCO|UNESCO]]-designated [[:en:World Heritage|World Heritage]] [[:en:Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range|Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The [[Kumano Kodō]] route connects it to other sites under the same classification, which are primarily located in [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan. The four sites on the route, classified as pilgrimage destinations and World Heritage Sites, are: 1) Nachi Taisha; 2) [[Kumano Hongū Taisha|Hongū Taisha]]; 3) [[Kumano Hayatama Taisha|Hayatama Taisha]]; 4) [[:en:Mount Kōya|Koya-san]].
Kumano Nachi Taisha is also one of the three sacred [[Kumano shrine|Kumano Sanzan]] shrines: # Kumano Nachi Taisha # [[Kumano Hongū Taisha]] # [[Kumano Hayatama Taisha]] This classification is based mostly in Japanese history, as pilgrims would travel to all three sites to complete their pilgrimage.
Kumano Nachi Taisha is an example of Buddhist and Shinto [[:en:Syncretism|Syncretism]] ([[Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu shūgō]]) nestled in the Kii Mountains, near Kii Katsuura, Japan. Cedar forests surround the site. The 133-meter [[Nachi Falls|Nachi Waterfall]], worshiped at the Hiryū Shrine near Kumano Nachi Taisha, is believed to be inhabited by a ''[[kami]]'' called Hiryū [[Gongen]].<ref name="nachi">{{cite web|url=http://www.kkr.mlit.go.jp/wakayama/rekishi/disco131.html|title=Hiryū Gongen|last=Kamizaka|first=Jirō|publisher=Ministry of Land, Infrastructure and Transport - Kinki Regional Development Bureau|language=ja|access-date=28 March 2010|archive-date=23 July 2011|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110723162553/http://www.kkr.mlit.go.jp/wakayama/rekishi/disco131.html|url-status=dead}}</ref> Also, there is a sacred tree at this site, the Sacred [[:en:Camphor laurel|Camphor Tree]], located between the Nachi Shrine (''[[Heiden (Shinto)|heiden]]'') and [[Seiganto-ji|Seigantoji temple]]. It is 850 years old and is said to have been planted by [[:en:Taira no Shigemori|Taira-no-Shigemori]] (1138–1179). The straw rope (''[[:en:Shimenawa|Shimenawa]]'') and paper flags show that this tree has been sanctified as a ''kami''. The tree is alive with moss and ferns and other small plants growing on its ancient limbs. It is possible to enter the base of the tree, where there is a small altar for making offerings, and come out above ground level.<ref>{{Cite web|url = http://sacredjapan.com/Temple%2001/Nachi%20Taisha.htm|title = Nachi Taisha-Sacred Camphor Tree|access-date = October 9, 2014|website = Sacred Japan|last = Juno|first = Cate Kodo|archive-date = March 3, 2016|archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20160303212124/http://sacredjapan.com/Temple%2001/Nachi%20Taisha.htm|url-status = dead}}</ref>
Nachi-no-Hi Matsuri Fire Festival, performed on [[:en:July 14|July 14]], is the major festival of Kumano Nachi Taisha. It is a fire festival in which six-meter-high portable shrines symbolically representing the purification of the waterfall with the fires from oversized torches is laboriously carried by men dressed in white.<ref>{{Cite web|url = http://www.tb-kumano.jp/en/world-heritage/kumano-nachi-taisha/|title = Kumano Nachi Taisha 熊野那智大社|access-date = October 9, 2014|website = Sacred Kumano|publisher = Tanabe City Kumano Tourism Bureau}}</ref>
==See also== * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] * [[:en:Twenty-Two Shrines|Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]
==References== {{Reflist}}
==External links== *[http://www.tb-kumano.jp/en/index.html#2 Tanabe City Kumano Tourism Bureau] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110925191301/http://www.tb-kumano.jp/en/index.html#2 |date=2011-09-25 }} *[http://www.kumadoco.net/kodo_eng/about/index.html#no2 Description of the sites] **[https://web.archive.org/web/20110610222210/http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/sekaiisan/english/e-kumano_si.html Wakayama Prefecture webpage about Kumano Nachi Taisha] *[http://www.nachikan.jp/en/ Nachi-Katsuura Town Tourism Association]
==Gallery== <gallery perrow="4" widths="170px" heights="130px"> Nachi-san Pagoda (Japan 2007 061).jpg|Nachi-san Pagoda Nachi Taisha's main temple (Japan 2007 082).jpg|Nachi Taisha's main temple Nachi waterfall (Japan 2007 047).jpg|Nachi waterfall Sanjūdō pagoda (Japan 2007 069).jpg|Sanjūdō pagoda, Nachi Taisha
</gallery>

It is possible to enter the base of the tree, where there is a small altar for making offerings, and come out above ground level.<ref>{{Cite web|url = http://sacredjapan.com/Temple%2001/Nachi%20Taisha.htm|title = Nachi Taisha-Sacred Camphor Tree|access-date = October 9, 2014|website = Sacred Japan|last = Juno|first = Cate Kodo|archive-date = March 3, 2016|archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20160303212124/http://sacredjapan.com/Temple%2001/Nachi%20Taisha.htm|url-status = dead}}</ref>

revision.comment: Text replacement - "Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrine" to "Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines"
revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q710359]] Wikidata name: Kumano Nachi Taisha [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:43, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Nachikatsuura, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = {{Nihongo|Kumano Nachi Taisha|熊野那智大社}} | image = Kumano-Nachi-shrine002.JPG | caption = Kumano Nachi Taisha's ''[[Heiden (Shinto)|heiden]]'' | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|33|40|07|N|135|53|26|E|display=title,inline|region:JP_type:landmark_source:dewiki}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Fusumi no kami (Izanami no mikoto) and others<ref>Cali, Joseph. Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion. — Honolulu, 2013. — pp. 253-256.</ref> | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = [[:en:Nachikatsuura|Nachikatsuura]], [[Higashimuro District, Wakayama|Higashimuro District]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }}
{{Nihongo|'''Kumano Nachi Taisha'''|熊野那智大社}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] and part of the [[:en:UNESCO|UNESCO]]-designated [[:en:World Heritage|World Heritage]] [[:en:Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range|Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The [[Kumano Kodō]] route connects it to other sites under the same classification, which are primarily located in [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan. The four sites on the route, classified as pilgrimage destinations and World Heritage Sites, are: 1) Nachi Taisha; 2) [[Kumano Hongū Taisha|Hongū Taisha]]; 3) [[Kumano Hayatama Taisha|Hayatama Taisha]]; 4) [[:en:Mount Kōya|Koya-san]].
Kumano Nachi Taisha is also one of the three sacred [[Kumano shrine|Kumano Sanzan]] shrines: # Kumano Nachi Taisha # [[Kumano Hongū Taisha]] # [[Kumano Hayatama Taisha]] This classification is based mostly in Japanese history, as pilgrims would travel to all three sites to complete their pilgrimage.
Kumano Nachi Taisha is an example of Buddhist and Shinto [[:en:Syncretism|Syncretism]] ([[Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu shūgō]]) nestled in the Kii Mountains, near Kii Katsuura, Japan. Cedar forests surround the site. The 133-meter [[Nachi Falls|Nachi Waterfall]], worshiped at the Hiryū Shrine near Kumano Nachi Taisha, is believed to be inhabited by a ''[[kami]]'' called Hiryū [[Gongen]].<ref name="nachi">{{cite web|url=http://www.kkr.mlit.go.jp/wakayama/rekishi/disco131.html|title=Hiryū Gongen|last=Kamizaka|first=Jirō|publisher=Ministry of Land, Infrastructure and Transport - Kinki Regional Development Bureau|language=ja|access-date=28 March 2010|archive-date=23 July 2011|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110723162553/http://www.kkr.mlit.go.jp/wakayama/rekishi/disco131.html|url-status=dead}}</ref> Also, there is a sacred tree at this site, the Sacred [[:en:Camphor laurel|Camphor Tree]], located between the Nachi Shrine (''[[Heiden (Shinto)|heiden]]'') and [[Seiganto-ji|Seigantoji temple]]. It is 850 years old and is said to have been planted by [[:en:Taira no Shigemori|Taira-no-Shigemori]] (1138–1179). The straw rope (''[[:en:Shimenawa|Shimenawa]]'') and paper flags show that this tree has been sanctified as a ''kami''. The tree is alive with moss and ferns and other small plants growing on its ancient limbs. It is possible to enter the base of the tree, where there is a small altar for making offerings, and come out above ground level.<ref>{{Cite web|url = http://sacredjapan.com/Temple%2001/Nachi%20Taisha.htm|title = Nachi Taisha-Sacred Camphor Tree|access-date = October 9, 2014|website = Sacred Japan|last = Juno|first = Cate Kodo|archive-date = March 3, 2016|archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20160303212124/http://sacredjapan.com/Temple%2001/Nachi%20Taisha.htm|url-status = dead}}</ref>
Nachi-no-Hi Matsuri Fire Festival, performed on [[:en:July 14|July 14]], is the major festival of Kumano Nachi Taisha. It is a fire festival in which six-meter-high portable shrines symbolically representing the purification of the waterfall with the fires from oversized torches is laboriously carried by men dressed in white.<ref>{{Cite web|url = http://www.tb-kumano.jp/en/world-heritage/kumano-nachi-taisha/|title = Kumano Nachi Taisha 熊野那智大社|access-date = October 9, 2014|website = Sacred Kumano|publisher = Tanabe City Kumano Tourism Bureau}}</ref>
==See also== * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] * [[:en:Twenty-Two Shrines|Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]
==References== {{Reflist}}
==External links== *[http://www.tb-kumano.jp/en/index.html#2 Tanabe City Kumano Tourism Bureau] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110925191301/http://www.tb-kumano.jp/en/index.html#2 |date=2011-09-25 }} *[http://www.kumadoco.net/kodo_eng/about/index.html#no2 Description of the sites] **[https://web.archive.org/web/20110610222210/http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/sekaiisan/english/e-kumano_si.html Wakayama Prefecture webpage about Kumano Nachi Taisha] *[http://www.nachikan.jp/en/ Nachi-Katsuura Town Tourism Association]
==Gallery== <gallery perrow="4" widths="170px" heights="130px"> Nachi-san Pagoda (Japan 2007 061).jpg|Nachi-san Pagoda Nachi Taisha's main temple (Japan 2007 082).jpg|Nachi Taisha's main temple Nachi waterfall (Japan 2007 047).jpg|Nachi waterfall Sanjūdō pagoda (Japan 2007 069).jpg|Sanjūdō pagoda, Nachi Taisha
</gallery>

Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion. — Honolulu, 2013. — pp. 253-256.</ref> | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = [[:en:Nachikatsuura|Nachikatsuura]], [[Higashimuro District, Wakayama|Higashimuro District]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }}
{{Nihongo|'''Kumano Nachi Taisha'''|熊野那智大社}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] and part of the [[:en:UNESCO|UNESCO]]-designated [[:en:World Heritage|World Heritage]] [[:en:Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range|Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The [[Kumano Kodō]] route connects it to other sites under the same classification, which are primarily located in [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan. The four sites on the route, classified as pilgrimage destinations and World Heritage Sites, are: 1) Nachi Taisha; 2) [[Kumano Hongū Taisha|Hongū Taisha]]; 3) [[Kumano Hayatama Taisha|Hayatama Taisha]]; 4) [[:en:Mount Kōya|Koya-san]].
Kumano Nachi Taisha is also one of the three sacred [[Kumano shrine|Kumano Sanzan]] shrines: # Kumano Nachi Taisha # [[Kumano Hongū Taisha]] # [[Kumano Hayatama Taisha]] This classification is based mostly in Japanese history, as pilgrims would travel to all three sites to complete their pilgrimage.
Kumano Nachi Taisha is an example of Buddhist and Shinto [[:en:Syncretism|Syncretism]] ([[Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu shūgō]]) nestled in the Kii Mountains, near Kii Katsuura, Japan. Cedar forests surround the site. The 133-meter [[Nachi Falls|Nachi Waterfall]], worshiped at the Hiryū Shrine near Kumano Nachi Taisha, is believed to be inhabited by a ''[[kami]]'' called Hiryū [[Gongen]].<ref name="nachi">{{cite web|url=http://www.kkr.mlit.go.jp/wakayama/rekishi/disco131.html|title=Hiryū Gongen|last=Kamizaka|first=Jirō|publisher=Ministry of Land, Infrastructure and Transport - Kinki Regional Development Bureau|language=ja|access-date=28 March 2010|archive-date=23 July 2011|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110723162553/http://www.kkr.mlit.go.jp/wakayama/rekishi/disco131.html|url-status=dead}}</ref> Also, there is a sacred tree at this site, the Sacred [[:en:Camphor laurel|Camphor Tree]], located between the Nachi Shrine (''[[Heiden (Shinto)|heiden]]'') and [[Seiganto-ji|Seigantoji temple]]. It is 850 years old and is said to have been planted by [[:en:Taira no Shigemori|Taira-no-Shigemori]] (1138–1179). The straw rope (''[[:en:Shimenawa|Shimenawa]]'') and paper flags show that this tree has been sanctified as a ''kami''. The tree is alive with moss and ferns and other small plants growing on its ancient limbs. It is possible to enter the base of the tree, where there is a small altar for making offerings, and come out above ground level.<ref>{{Cite web|url = http://sacredjapan.com/Temple%2001/Nachi%20Taisha.htm|title = Nachi Taisha-Sacred Camphor Tree|access-date = October 9, 2014|website = Sacred Japan|last = Juno|first = Cate Kodo|archive-date = March 3, 2016|archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20160303212124/http://sacredjapan.com/Temple%2001/Nachi%20Taisha.htm|url-status = dead}}</ref>
Nachi-no-Hi Matsuri Fire Festival, performed on [[:en:July 14|July 14]], is the major festival of Kumano Nachi Taisha. It is a fire festival in which six-meter-high portable shrines symbolically representing the purification of the waterfall with the fires from oversized torches is laboriously carried by men dressed in white.<ref>{{Cite web|url = http://www.tb-kumano.jp/en/world-heritage/kumano-nachi-taisha/|title = Kumano Nachi Taisha 熊野那智大社|access-date = October 9, 2014|website = Sacred Kumano|publisher = Tanabe City Kumano Tourism Bureau}}</ref>

The [[Kumano Kodō]] route connects it to other sites under the same classification, which are primarily located in [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan. The four sites on the route, classified as pilgrimage destinations and World Heritage Sites, are: 1) Nachi Taisha; 2) [[Kumano Hongū Taisha|Hongū Taisha]]; 3) [[Kumano Hayatama Taisha|Hayatama Taisha]]; 4) [[:en:Mount Kōya|Koya-san]].
Kumano Nachi Taisha is also one of the three sacred [[Kumano shrine|Kumano Sanzan]] shrines: # Kumano Nachi Taisha # [[Kumano Hongū Taisha]] # [[Kumano Hayatama Taisha]] This classification is based mostly in Japanese history, as pilgrims would travel to all three sites to complete their pilgrimage.
Kumano Nachi Taisha is an example of Buddhist and Shinto [[:en:Syncretism|Syncretism]] ([[Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu shūgō]]) nestled in the Kii Mountains, near Kii Katsuura, Japan. Cedar forests surround the site. The 133-meter [[Nachi Falls|Nachi Waterfall]], worshiped at the Hiryū Shrine near Kumano Nachi Taisha, is believed to be inhabited by a ''[[kami]]'' called Hiryū [[Gongen]].<ref name="nachi">{{cite web|url=http://www.kkr.mlit.go.jp/wakayama/rekishi/disco131.html|title=Hiryū Gongen|last=Kamizaka|first=Jirō|publisher=Ministry of Land, Infrastructure and Transport - Kinki Regional Development Bureau|language=ja|access-date=28 March 2010|archive-date=23 July 2011|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110723162553/http://www.kkr.mlit.go.jp/wakayama/rekishi/disco131.html|url-status=dead}}</ref> Also, there is a sacred tree at this site, the Sacred [[:en:Camphor laurel|Camphor Tree]], located between the Nachi Shrine (''[[Heiden (Shinto)|heiden]]'') and [[Seiganto-ji|Seigantoji temple]]. It is 850 years old and is said to have been planted by [[:en:Taira no Shigemori|Taira-no-Shigemori]] (1138–1179). The straw rope (''[[:en:Shimenawa|Shimenawa]]'') and paper flags show that this tree has been sanctified as a ''kami''. The tree is alive with moss and ferns and other small plants growing on its ancient limbs. It is possible to enter the base of the tree, where there is a small altar for making offerings, and come out above ground level.<ref>{{Cite web|url = http://sacredjapan.com/Temple%2001/Nachi%20Taisha.htm|title = Nachi Taisha-Sacred Camphor Tree|access-date = October 9, 2014|website = Sacred Japan|last = Juno|first = Cate Kodo|archive-date = March 3, 2016|archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20160303212124/http://sacredjapan.com/Temple%2001/Nachi%20Taisha.htm|url-status = dead}}</ref>
Nachi-no-Hi Matsuri Fire Festival, performed on [[:en:July 14|July 14]], is the major festival of Kumano Nachi Taisha. It is a fire festival in which six-meter-high portable shrines symbolically representing the purification of the waterfall with the fires from oversized torches is laboriously carried by men dressed in white.<ref>{{Cite web|url = http://www.tb-kumano.jp/en/world-heritage/kumano-nachi-taisha/|title = Kumano Nachi Taisha 熊野那智大社|access-date = October 9, 2014|website = Sacred Kumano|publisher = Tanabe City Kumano Tourism Bureau}}</ref>
==See also== * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] * [[:en:Twenty-Two Shrines|Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]

The 133-meter [[Nachi Falls|Nachi Waterfall]], worshiped at the Hiryū Shrine near Kumano Nachi Taisha, is believed to be inhabited by a ''[[kami]]'' called Hiryū [[Gongen]].<ref name="nachi">{{cite web|url=http://www.kkr.mlit.go.jp/wakayama/rekishi/disco131.html|title=Hiryū Gongen|last=Kamizaka|first=Jirō|publisher=Ministry of Land, Infrastructure and Transport - Kinki Regional Development Bureau|language=ja|access-date=28 March 2010|archive-date=23 July 2011|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110723162553/http://www.kkr.mlit.go.jp/wakayama/rekishi/disco131.html|url-status=dead}}</ref> Also, there is a sacred tree at this site, the Sacred [[:en:Camphor laurel|Camphor Tree]], located between the Nachi Shrine (''[[Heiden (Shinto)|heiden]]'') and [[Seiganto-ji|Seigantoji temple]]. It is 850 years old and is said to have been planted by [[:en:Taira no Shigemori|Taira-no-Shigemori]] (1138–1179). The straw rope (''[[:en:Shimenawa|Shimenawa]]'') and paper flags show that this tree has been sanctified as a ''kami''. The tree is alive with moss and ferns and other small plants growing on its ancient limbs. It is possible to enter the base of the tree, where there is a small altar for making offerings, and come out above ground level.<ref>{{Cite web|url = http://sacredjapan.com/Temple%2001/Nachi%20Taisha.htm|title = Nachi Taisha-Sacred Camphor Tree|access-date = October 9, 2014|website = Sacred Japan|last = Juno|first = Cate Kodo|archive-date = March 3, 2016|archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20160303212124/http://sacredjapan.com/Temple%2001/Nachi%20Taisha.htm|url-status = dead}}</ref>
Nachi-no-Hi Matsuri Fire Festival, performed on [[:en:July 14|July 14]], is the major festival of Kumano Nachi Taisha. It is a fire festival in which six-meter-high portable shrines symbolically representing the purification of the waterfall with the fires from oversized torches is laboriously carried by men dressed in white.<ref>{{Cite web|url = http://www.tb-kumano.jp/en/world-heritage/kumano-nachi-taisha/|title = Kumano Nachi Taisha 熊野那智大社|access-date = October 9, 2014|website = Sacred Kumano|publisher = Tanabe City Kumano Tourism Bureau}}</ref>

{{Nihongo|'''Kumano Nachi Taisha'''|熊野那智大社}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] and part of the [[:en:UNESCO|UNESCO]]-designated [[:en:World Heritage|World Heritage]] [[:en:Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range|Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The [[Kumano Kodō]] route connects it to other sites under the same classification, which are primarily located in [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan. The four sites on the route, classified as pilgrimage destinations and World Heritage Sites, are: 1) Nachi Taisha; 2) [[Kumano Hongū Taisha|Hongū Taisha]]; 3) [[Kumano Hayatama Taisha|Hayatama Taisha]]; 4) [[:en:Mount Kōya|Koya-san]].
Kumano Nachi Taisha is also one of the three sacred [[Kumano shrine|Kumano Sanzan]] shrines: # Kumano Nachi Taisha # [[Kumano Hongū Taisha]] # [[Kumano Hayatama Taisha]] This classification is based mostly in Japanese history, as pilgrims would travel to all three sites to complete their pilgrimage.

The tree is alive with moss and ferns and other small plants growing on its ancient limbs. It is possible to enter the base of the tree, where there is a small altar for making offerings, and come out above ground level.<ref>{{Cite web|url = http://sacredjapan.com/Temple%2001/Nachi%20Taisha.htm|title = Nachi Taisha-Sacred Camphor Tree|access-date = October 9, 2014|website = Sacred Japan|last = Juno|first = Cate Kodo|archive-date = March 3, 2016|archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20160303212124/http://sacredjapan.com/Temple%2001/Nachi%20Taisha.htm|url-status = dead}}</ref>

Kumano Nachi Taisha is an example of Buddhist and Shinto [[:en:Syncretism|Syncretism]] ([[Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu shūgō]]) nestled in the Kii Mountains, near Kii Katsuura, Japan. Cedar forests surround the site. The 133-meter [[Nachi Falls|Nachi Waterfall]], worshiped at the Hiryū Shrine near Kumano Nachi Taisha, is believed to be inhabited by a ''[[kami]]'' called Hiryū [[Gongen]].<ref name="nachi">{{cite web|url=http://www.kkr.mlit.go.jp/wakayama/rekishi/disco131.html|title=Hiryū Gongen|last=Kamizaka|first=Jirō|publisher=Ministry of Land, Infrastructure and Transport - Kinki Regional Development Bureau|language=ja|access-date=28 March 2010|archive-date=23 July 2011|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110723162553/http://www.kkr.mlit.go.jp/wakayama/rekishi/disco131.html|url-status=dead}}</ref> Also, there is a sacred tree at this site, the Sacred [[:en:Camphor laurel|Camphor Tree]], located between the Nachi Shrine (''[[Heiden (Shinto)|heiden]]'') and [[Seiganto-ji|Seigantoji temple]]. It is 850 years old and is said to have been planted by [[:en:Taira no Shigemori|Taira-no-Shigemori]] (1138–1179). The straw rope (''[[:en:Shimenawa|Shimenawa]]'') and paper flags show that this tree has been sanctified as a ''kami''. The tree is alive with moss and ferns and other small plants growing on its ancient limbs. It is possible to enter the base of the tree, where there is a small altar for making offerings, and come out above ground level.<ref>{{Cite web|url = http://sacredjapan.com/Temple%2001/Nachi%20Taisha.htm|title = Nachi Taisha-Sacred Camphor Tree|access-date = October 9, 2014|website = Sacred Japan|last = Juno|first = Cate Kodo|archive-date = March 3, 2016|archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20160303212124/http://sacredjapan.com/Temple%2001/Nachi%20Taisha.htm|url-status = dead}}</ref>
Nachi-no-Hi Matsuri Fire Festival, performed on [[:en:July 14|July 14]], is the major festival of Kumano Nachi Taisha. It is a fire festival in which six-meter-high portable shrines symbolically representing the purification of the waterfall with the fires from oversized torches is laboriously carried by men dressed in white.<ref>{{Cite web|url = http://www.tb-kumano.jp/en/world-heritage/kumano-nachi-taisha/|title = Kumano Nachi Taisha 熊野那智大社|access-date = October 9, 2014|website = Sacred Kumano|publisher = Tanabe City Kumano Tourism Bureau}}</ref>

{{Nihongo|'''Kumano Nachi Taisha'''|熊野那智大社}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] and part of the [[:en:UNESCO|UNESCO]]-designated [[:en:World Heritage|World Heritage]] [[:en:Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range|Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]].
