Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion. — Honolulu, 2013. — pp. 253-256.</ref> | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = [[:en:Nachikatsuura|Nachikatsuura]], [[Higashimuro District, Wakayama|Higashimuro District]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }}
{{Nihongo|'''Kumano Nachi Taisha'''|熊野那智大社}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] and part of the [[:en:UNESCO|UNESCO]]-designated [[:en:World Heritage|World Heritage]] [[:en:Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range|Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The [[Kumano Kodō]] route connects it to other sites under the same classification, which are primarily located in [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan. The four sites on the route, classified as pilgrimage destinations and World Heritage Sites, are: 1) Nachi Taisha; 2) [[Kumano Hongū Taisha|Hongū Taisha]]; 3) [[Kumano Hayatama Taisha|Hayatama Taisha]]; 4) [[:en:Mount Kōya|Koya-san]].
Kumano Nachi Taisha is also one of the three sacred [[Kumano shrine|Kumano Sanzan]] shrines: # Kumano Nachi Taisha # [[Kumano Hongū Taisha]] # [[Kumano Hayatama Taisha]] This classification is based mostly in Japanese history, as pilgrims would travel to all three sites to complete their pilgrimage.
Kumano Nachi Taisha is an example of Buddhist and Shinto [[:en:Syncretism|Syncretism]] ([[Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu shūgō]]) nestled in the Kii Mountains, near Kii Katsuura, Japan. Cedar forests surround the site. The 133-meter [[Nachi Falls|Nachi Waterfall]], worshiped at the Hiryū Shrine near Kumano Nachi Taisha, is believed to be inhabited by a ''[[kami]]'' called Hiryū [[Gongen]].<ref name="nachi">{{cite web|url=http://www.kkr.mlit.go.jp/wakayama/rekishi/disco131.html|title=Hiryū Gongen|last=Kamizaka|first=Jirō|publisher=Ministry of Land, Infrastructure and Transport - Kinki Regional Development Bureau|language=ja|access-date=28 March 2010|archive-date=23 July 2011|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110723162553/http://www.kkr.mlit.go.jp/wakayama/rekishi/disco131.html|url-status=dead}}</ref> Also, there is a sacred tree at this site, the Sacred [[:en:Camphor laurel|Camphor Tree]], located between the Nachi Shrine (''[[Heiden (Shinto)|heiden]]'') and [[Seiganto-ji|Seigantoji temple]]. It is 850 years old and is said to have been planted by [[:en:Taira no Shigemori|Taira-no-Shigemori]] (1138–1179). The straw rope (''[[:en:Shimenawa|Shimenawa]]'') and paper flags show that this tree has been sanctified as a ''kami''. The tree is alive with moss and ferns and other small plants growing on its ancient limbs. It is possible to enter the base of the tree, where there is a small altar for making offerings, and come out above ground level.<ref>{{Cite web|url = http://sacredjapan.com/Temple%2001/Nachi%20Taisha.htm|title = Nachi Taisha-Sacred Camphor Tree|access-date = October 9, 2014|website = Sacred Japan|last = Juno|first = Cate Kodo|archive-date = March 3, 2016|archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20160303212124/http://sacredjapan.com/Temple%2001/Nachi%20Taisha.htm|url-status = dead}}</ref>
Nachi-no-Hi Matsuri Fire Festival, performed on [[:en:July 14|July 14]], is the major festival of Kumano Nachi Taisha. It is a fire festival in which six-meter-high portable shrines symbolically representing the purification of the waterfall with the fires from oversized torches is laboriously carried by men dressed in white.<ref>{{Cite web|url = http://www.tb-kumano.jp/en/world-heritage/kumano-nachi-taisha/|title = Kumano Nachi Taisha 熊野那智大社|access-date = October 9, 2014|website = Sacred Kumano|publisher = Tanabe City Kumano Tourism Bureau}}</ref>
==See also== * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] * [[:en:Twenty-Two Shrines|Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]
==References== {{Reflist}}
==External links== *[http://www.tb-kumano.jp/en/index.html#2 Tanabe City Kumano Tourism Bureau] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110925191301/http://www.tb-kumano.jp/en/index.html#2 |date=2011-09-25 }} *[http://www.kumadoco.net/kodo_eng/about/index.html#no2 Description of the sites] **[https://web.archive.org/web/20110610222210/http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/sekaiisan/english/e-kumano_si.html Wakayama Prefecture webpage about Kumano Nachi Taisha] *[http://www.nachikan.jp/en/ Nachi-Katsuura Town Tourism Association]
==Gallery== <gallery perrow="4" widths="170px" heights="130px"> Nachi-san Pagoda (Japan 2007 061).jpg|Nachi-san Pagoda Nachi Taisha's main temple (Japan 2007 082).jpg|Nachi Taisha's main temple Nachi waterfall (Japan 2007 047).jpg|Nachi waterfall Sanjūdō pagoda (Japan 2007 069).jpg|Sanjūdō pagoda, Nachi Taisha
</gallery>

It is a fire festival in which six-meter-high portable shrines symbolically representing the purification of the waterfall with the fires from oversized torches is laboriously carried by men dressed in white.<ref>{{Cite web|url = http://www.tb-kumano.jp/en/world-heritage/kumano-nachi-taisha/|title = Kumano Nachi Taisha 熊野那智大社|access-date = October 9, 2014|website = Sacred Kumano|publisher = Tanabe City Kumano Tourism Bureau}}</ref>

Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion. — Honolulu, 2013. — pp. 253-256.</ref> | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = [[:en:Nachikatsuura|Nachikatsuura]], [[Higashimuro District, Wakayama|Higashimuro District]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }}
{{Nihongo|'''Kumano Nachi Taisha'''|熊野那智大社}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] and part of the [[:en:UNESCO|UNESCO]]-designated [[:en:World Heritage|World Heritage]] [[:en:Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range|Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The [[Kumano Kodō]] route connects it to other sites under the same classification, which are primarily located in [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan. The four sites on the route, classified as pilgrimage destinations and World Heritage Sites, are: 1) Nachi Taisha; 2) [[Kumano Hongū Taisha|Hongū Taisha]]; 3) [[Kumano Hayatama Taisha|Hayatama Taisha]]; 4) [[:en:Mount Kōya|Koya-san]].
Kumano Nachi Taisha is also one of the three sacred [[Kumano shrine|Kumano Sanzan]] shrines: # Kumano Nachi Taisha # [[Kumano Hongū Taisha]] # [[Kumano Hayatama Taisha]] This classification is based mostly in Japanese history, as pilgrims would travel to all three sites to complete their pilgrimage.
Kumano Nachi Taisha is an example of Buddhist and Shinto [[:en:Syncretism|Syncretism]] ([[Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu shūgō]]) nestled in the Kii Mountains, near Kii Katsuura, Japan. Cedar forests surround the site. The 133-meter [[Nachi Falls|Nachi Waterfall]], worshiped at the Hiryū Shrine near Kumano Nachi Taisha, is believed to be inhabited by a ''[[kami]]'' called Hiryū [[Gongen]].<ref name="nachi">{{cite web|url=http://www.kkr.mlit.go.jp/wakayama/rekishi/disco131.html|title=Hiryū Gongen|last=Kamizaka|first=Jirō|publisher=Ministry of Land, Infrastructure and Transport - Kinki Regional Development Bureau|language=ja|access-date=28 March 2010|archive-date=23 July 2011|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110723162553/http://www.kkr.mlit.go.jp/wakayama/rekishi/disco131.html|url-status=dead}}</ref> Also, there is a sacred tree at this site, the Sacred [[:en:Camphor laurel|Camphor Tree]], located between the Nachi Shrine (''[[Heiden (Shinto)|heiden]]'') and [[Seiganto-ji|Seigantoji temple]]. It is 850 years old and is said to have been planted by [[:en:Taira no Shigemori|Taira-no-Shigemori]] (1138–1179). The straw rope (''[[:en:Shimenawa|Shimenawa]]'') and paper flags show that this tree has been sanctified as a ''kami''. The tree is alive with moss and ferns and other small plants growing on its ancient limbs. It is possible to enter the base of the tree, where there is a small altar for making offerings, and come out above ground level.<ref>{{Cite web|url = http://sacredjapan.com/Temple%2001/Nachi%20Taisha.htm|title = Nachi Taisha-Sacred Camphor Tree|access-date = October 9, 2014|website = Sacred Japan|last = Juno|first = Cate Kodo|archive-date = March 3, 2016|archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20160303212124/http://sacredjapan.com/Temple%2001/Nachi%20Taisha.htm|url-status = dead}}</ref>
Nachi-no-Hi Matsuri Fire Festival, performed on [[:en:July 14|July 14]], is the major festival of Kumano Nachi Taisha. It is a fire festival in which six-meter-high portable shrines symbolically representing the purification of the waterfall with the fires from oversized torches is laboriously carried by men dressed in white.<ref>{{Cite web|url = http://www.tb-kumano.jp/en/world-heritage/kumano-nachi-taisha/|title = Kumano Nachi Taisha 熊野那智大社|access-date = October 9, 2014|website = Sacred Kumano|publisher = Tanabe City Kumano Tourism Bureau}}</ref>

Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion. — Honolulu, 2013. — pp. 253-256.</ref> | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = [[:en:Nachikatsuura|Nachikatsuura]], [[Higashimuro District, Wakayama|Higashimuro District]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }}
{{Nihongo|'''Kumano Nachi Taisha'''|熊野那智大社}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] and part of the [[:en:UNESCO|UNESCO]]-designated [[:en:World Heritage|World Heritage]] [[:en:Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range|Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The [[Kumano Kodō]] route connects it to other sites under the same classification, which are primarily located in [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan. The four sites on the route, classified as pilgrimage destinations and World Heritage Sites, are: 1) Nachi Taisha; 2) [[Kumano Hongū Taisha|Hongū Taisha]]; 3) [[Kumano Hayatama Taisha|Hayatama Taisha]]; 4) [[:en:Mount Kōya|Koya-san]].
Kumano Nachi Taisha is also one of the three sacred [[Kumano shrine|Kumano Sanzan]] shrines: # Kumano Nachi Taisha # [[Kumano Hongū Taisha]] # [[Kumano Hayatama Taisha]] This classification is based mostly in Japanese history, as pilgrims would travel to all three sites to complete their pilgrimage.
Kumano Nachi Taisha is an example of Buddhist and Shinto [[:en:Syncretism|Syncretism]] ([[Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu shūgō]]) nestled in the Kii Mountains, near Kii Katsuura, Japan. Cedar forests surround the site. The 133-meter [[Nachi Falls|Nachi Waterfall]], worshiped at the Hiryū Shrine near Kumano Nachi Taisha, is believed to be inhabited by a ''[[kami]]'' called Hiryū [[Gongen]].<ref name="nachi">{{cite web|url=http://www.kkr.mlit.go.jp/wakayama/rekishi/disco131.html|title=Hiryū Gongen|last=Kamizaka|first=Jirō|publisher=Ministry of Land, Infrastructure and Transport - Kinki Regional Development Bureau|language=ja|access-date=28 March 2010|archive-date=23 July 2011|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110723162553/http://www.kkr.mlit.go.jp/wakayama/rekishi/disco131.html|url-status=dead}}</ref> Also, there is a sacred tree at this site, the Sacred [[:en:Camphor laurel|Camphor Tree]], located between the Nachi Shrine (''[[Heiden (Shinto)|heiden]]'') and [[Seiganto-ji|Seigantoji temple]]. It is 850 years old and is said to have been planted by [[:en:Taira no Shigemori|Taira-no-Shigemori]] (1138–1179). The straw rope (''[[:en:Shimenawa|Shimenawa]]'') and paper flags show that this tree has been sanctified as a ''kami''. The tree is alive with moss and ferns and other small plants growing on its ancient limbs. It is possible to enter the base of the tree, where there is a small altar for making offerings, and come out above ground level.<ref>{{Cite web|url = http://sacredjapan.com/Temple%2001/Nachi%20Taisha.htm|title = Nachi Taisha-Sacred Camphor Tree|access-date = October 9, 2014|website = Sacred Japan|last = Juno|first = Cate Kodo|archive-date = March 3, 2016|archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20160303212124/http://sacredjapan.com/Temple%2001/Nachi%20Taisha.htm|url-status = dead}}</ref>
Nachi-no-Hi Matsuri Fire Festival, performed on [[:en:July 14|July 14]], is the major festival of Kumano Nachi Taisha. It is a fire festival in which six-meter-high portable shrines symbolically representing the purification of the waterfall with the fires from oversized torches is laboriously carried by men dressed in white.<ref>{{Cite web|url = http://www.tb-kumano.jp/en/world-heritage/kumano-nachi-taisha/|title = Kumano Nachi Taisha 熊野那智大社|access-date = October 9, 2014|website = Sacred Kumano|publisher = Tanabe City Kumano Tourism Bureau}}</ref>
==See also== * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] * [[:en:Twenty-Two Shrines|Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]

The 133-meter [[Nachi Falls|Nachi Waterfall]], worshiped at the Hiryū Shrine near Kumano Nachi Taisha, is believed to be inhabited by a ''[[kami]]'' called Hiryū [[Gongen]].<ref name="nachi">{{cite web|url=http://www.kkr.mlit.go.jp/wakayama/rekishi/disco131.html|title=Hiryū Gongen|last=Kamizaka|first=Jirō|publisher=Ministry of Land, Infrastructure and Transport - Kinki Regional Development Bureau|language=ja|access-date=28 March 2010|archive-date=23 July 2011|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110723162553/http://www.kkr.mlit.go.jp/wakayama/rekishi/disco131.html|url-status=dead}}</ref> Also, there is a sacred tree at this site, the Sacred [[:en:Camphor laurel|Camphor Tree]], located between the Nachi Shrine (''[[Heiden (Shinto)|heiden]]'') and [[Seiganto-ji|Seigantoji temple]]. It is 850 years old and is said to have been planted by {{ill|Taira no Shigemori|en|Taira no Shigemori|WD=Q1184985|lt=Taira-no-Shigemori}} (1138–1179). The straw rope (''{{ill|Shimenawa|en|Shimenawa|WD=Q1887421|lt=Shimenawa}}'') and paper flags show that this tree has been sanctified as a ''kami''. The tree is alive with moss and ferns and other small plants growing on its ancient limbs. It is possible to enter the base of the tree, where there is a small altar for making offerings, and come out above ground level.<ref>{{Cite web|url = http://sacredjapan.com/Temple%2001/Nachi%20Taisha.htm|title = Nachi Taisha-Sacred Camphor Tree|access-date = October 9, 2014|website = Sacred Japan|last = Juno|first = Cate Kodo|archive-date = March 3, 2016|archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20160303212124/http://sacredjapan.com/Temple%2001/Nachi%20Taisha.htm|url-status = dead}}</ref>
Nachi-no-Hi Matsuri Fire Festival, performed on {{ill|July 14|en|July 14|WD=Q2691|lt=July 14}}, is the major festival of Kumano Nachi Taisha. It is a fire festival in which six-meter-high portable shrines symbolically representing the purification of the waterfall with the fires from oversized torches is laboriously carried by men dressed in white.<ref>{{Cite web|url = http://www.tb-kumano.jp/en/world-heritage/kumano-nachi-taisha/|title = Kumano Nachi Taisha 熊野那智大社|access-date = October 9, 2014|website = Sacred Kumano|publisher = Tanabe City Kumano Tourism Bureau}}</ref>
==See also== * {{ill|List of Shinto shrines|en|List of Shinto shrines|WD=Q120679573|lt=List of Shinto shrines}} * {{ill|Twenty-Two Shrines|en|Twenty-Two Shrines|WD=Q215320|lt=Twenty-Two Shrines}} * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]

Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion. — Honolulu, 2013. — pp. 253-256.</ref> | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = {{ill|Nachikatsuura|en|Nachikatsuura|WD=Q1346730|lt=Nachikatsuura}}, [[Higashimuro District, Wakayama|Higashimuro District]], {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314|lt=Wakayama Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}} | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }}
{{Nihongo|'''Kumano Nachi Taisha'''|熊野那智大社}} is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} and part of the {{ill|UNESCO|en|UNESCO|WD=Q7809|lt=UNESCO}}-designated [[:en:World Heritage|World Heritage]] {{ill|Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range|en|Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range|WD=Q611345|lt=Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. The [[Kumano Kodō]] route connects it to other sites under the same classification, which are primarily located in {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314|lt=Wakayama Prefecture}}, Japan. The four sites on the route, classified as pilgrimage destinations and World Heritage Sites, are: 1) Nachi Taisha; 2) [[Kumano Hongū Taisha|Hongū Taisha]]; 3) [[Kumano Hayatama Taisha|Hayatama Taisha]]; 4) {{ill|Mount Kōya|en|Mount Kōya|WD=Q535065|lt=Koya-san}}.
Kumano Nachi Taisha is also one of the three sacred [[Kumano shrine|Kumano Sanzan]] shrines: # Kumano Nachi Taisha # [[Kumano Hongū Taisha]] # [[Kumano Hayatama Taisha]] This classification is based mostly in Japanese history, as pilgrims would travel to all three sites to complete their pilgrimage.
Kumano Nachi Taisha is an example of Buddhist and Shinto {{ill|Syncretism|en|Syncretism|WD=Q172904|lt=Syncretism}} ([[Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu shūgō]]) nestled in the Kii Mountains, near Kii Katsuura, Japan. Cedar forests surround the site. The 133-meter [[Nachi Falls|Nachi Waterfall]], worshiped at the Hiryū Shrine near Kumano Nachi Taisha, is believed to be inhabited by a ''[[kami]]'' called Hiryū [[Gongen]].<ref name="nachi">{{cite web|url=http://www.kkr.mlit.go.jp/wakayama/rekishi/disco131.html|title=Hiryū Gongen|last=Kamizaka|first=Jirō|publisher=Ministry of Land, Infrastructure and Transport - Kinki Regional Development Bureau|language=ja|access-date=28 March 2010|archive-date=23 July 2011|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110723162553/http://www.kkr.mlit.go.jp/wakayama/rekishi/disco131.html|url-status=dead}}</ref> Also, there is a sacred tree at this site, the Sacred [[:en:Camphor laurel|Camphor Tree]], located between the Nachi Shrine (''[[Heiden (Shinto)|heiden]]'') and [[Seiganto-ji|Seigantoji temple]]. It is 850 years old and is said to have been planted by {{ill|Taira no Shigemori|en|Taira no Shigemori|WD=Q1184985|lt=Taira-no-Shigemori}} (1138–1179). The straw rope (''{{ill|Shimenawa|en|Shimenawa|WD=Q1887421|lt=Shimenawa}}'') and paper flags show that this tree has been sanctified as a ''kami''. The tree is alive with moss and ferns and other small plants growing on its ancient limbs. It is possible to enter the base of the tree, where there is a small altar for making offerings, and come out above ground level.<ref>{{Cite web|url = http://sacredjapan.com/Temple%2001/Nachi%20Taisha.htm|title = Nachi Taisha-Sacred Camphor Tree|access-date = October 9, 2014|website = Sacred Japan|last = Juno|first = Cate Kodo|archive-date = March 3, 2016|archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20160303212124/http://sacredjapan.com/Temple%2001/Nachi%20Taisha.htm|url-status = dead}}</ref>
Nachi-no-Hi Matsuri Fire Festival, performed on {{ill|July 14|en|July 14|WD=Q2691|lt=July 14}}, is the major festival of Kumano Nachi Taisha. It is a fire festival in which six-meter-high portable shrines symbolically representing the purification of the waterfall with the fires from oversized torches is laboriously carried by men dressed in white.<ref>{{Cite web|url = http://www.tb-kumano.jp/en/world-heritage/kumano-nachi-taisha/|title = Kumano Nachi Taisha 熊野那智大社|access-date = October 9, 2014|website = Sacred Kumano|publisher = Tanabe City Kumano Tourism Bureau}}</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kumano-Nachi-shrine]][[de:Kumano Nachi-Taisha]][[el:Κουμάνο Νάτσι Τάισα]][[en:Kumano Nachi Taisha]][[eu:Kumano Nachi Taisha]][[fa:کومانو ناچی تایشا]][[fr:Kumano Nachi-taisha]][[it:Kumano Nachi Taisha]][[ja:熊野那智大社]][[la:Kumano Nachi Taisha]][[lt:Načio maldykla]][[ru:Кумано нати тайся]][[tr:Kumano Nachi-Taisha]][[zh:熊野那智大社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kumano-Nachi-shrine]][[de:Kumano Nachi-Taisha]][[el:Κουμάνο Νάτσι Τάισα]][[en:Kumano Nachi Taisha]][[eu:Kumano Nachi Taisha]][[fa:کومانو ناچی تایشا]][[fr:Kumano Nachi-taisha]][[it:Kumano Nachi Taisha]][[ja:熊野那智大社]][[la:Kumano Nachi Taisha]][[lt:Načio maldykla]][[ru:Кумано нати тайся]][[tr:Kumano Nachi-Taisha]][[zh:熊野那智大社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Kumano-Nachi-shrine]][[de:Kumano Nachi-Taisha]][[el:Κουμάνο Νάτσι Τάισα]][[en:Kumano Nachi Taisha]][[eu:Kumano Nachi Taisha]][[fa:کومانو ناچی تایشا]][[fr:Kumano Nachi-taisha]][[it:Kumano Nachi Taisha]][[ja:熊野那智大社]][[la:Kumano Nachi Taisha]][[lt:Načio maldykla]][[ru:Кумано нати тайся]][[tr:Kumano Nachi-Taisha]][[zh:熊野那智大社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q710359]] Wikidata name: Kumano Nachi Taisha [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:43, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Nachikatsuura, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = {{Nihongo|Kumano Nachi Taisha|熊野那智大社}} | image = Kumano-Nachi-shrine002.JPG | caption = Kumano Nachi Taisha's ''[[Heiden (Shinto)|heiden]]'' | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|33|40|07|N|135|53|26|E|display=title,inline|region:JP_type:landmark_source:dewiki}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Fusumi no kami (Izanami no mikoto) and others<ref>Cali, Joseph. Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion. — Honolulu, 2013. — pp. 253-256.</ref> | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = {{ill|Nachikatsuura|en|Nachikatsuura|WD=Q1346730|lt=Nachikatsuura}}, [[Higashimuro District, Wakayama|Higashimuro District]], {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314|lt=Wakayama Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}} | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }}
{{Nihongo|'''Kumano Nachi Taisha'''|熊野那智大社}} is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} and part of the {{ill|UNESCO|en|UNESCO|WD=Q7809|lt=UNESCO}}-designated [[:en:World Heritage|World Heritage]] {{ill|Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range|en|Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range|WD=Q611345|lt=Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. The [[Kumano Kodō]] route connects it to other sites under the same classification, which are primarily located in {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314|lt=Wakayama Prefecture}}, Japan. The four sites on the route, classified as pilgrimage destinations and World Heritage Sites, are: 1) Nachi Taisha; 2) [[Kumano Hongū Taisha|Hongū Taisha]]; 3) [[Kumano Hayatama Taisha|Hayatama Taisha]]; 4) {{ill|Mount Kōya|en|Mount Kōya|WD=Q535065|lt=Koya-san}}.
Kumano Nachi Taisha is also one of the three sacred [[Kumano shrine|Kumano Sanzan]] shrines: # Kumano Nachi Taisha # [[Kumano Hongū Taisha]] # [[Kumano Hayatama Taisha]] This classification is based mostly in Japanese history, as pilgrims would travel to all three sites to complete their pilgrimage.
Kumano Nachi Taisha is an example of Buddhist and Shinto {{ill|Syncretism|en|Syncretism|WD=Q172904|lt=Syncretism}} ([[Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu shūgō]]) nestled in the Kii Mountains, near Kii Katsuura, Japan. Cedar forests surround the site. The 133-meter [[Nachi Falls|Nachi Waterfall]], worshiped at the Hiryū Shrine near Kumano Nachi Taisha, is believed to be inhabited by a ''[[kami]]'' called Hiryū [[Gongen]].<ref name="nachi">{{cite web|url=http://www.kkr.mlit.go.jp/wakayama/rekishi/disco131.html|title=Hiryū Gongen|last=Kamizaka|first=Jirō|publisher=Ministry of Land, Infrastructure and Transport - Kinki Regional Development Bureau|language=ja|access-date=28 March 2010|archive-date=23 July 2011|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110723162553/http://www.kkr.mlit.go.jp/wakayama/rekishi/disco131.html|url-status=dead}}</ref> Also, there is a sacred tree at this site, the Sacred [[:en:Camphor laurel|Camphor Tree]], located between the Nachi Shrine (''[[Heiden (Shinto)|heiden]]'') and [[Seiganto-ji|Seigantoji temple]]. It is 850 years old and is said to have been planted by {{ill|Taira no Shigemori|en|Taira no Shigemori|WD=Q1184985|lt=Taira-no-Shigemori}} (1138–1179). The straw rope (''{{ill|Shimenawa|en|Shimenawa|WD=Q1887421|lt=Shimenawa}}'') and paper flags show that this tree has been sanctified as a ''kami''. The tree is alive with moss and ferns and other small plants growing on its ancient limbs. It is possible to enter the base of the tree, where there is a small altar for making offerings, and come out above ground level.<ref>{{Cite web|url = http://sacredjapan.com/Temple%2001/Nachi%20Taisha.htm|title = Nachi Taisha-Sacred Camphor Tree|access-date = October 9, 2014|website = Sacred Japan|last = Juno|first = Cate Kodo|archive-date = March 3, 2016|archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20160303212124/http://sacredjapan.com/Temple%2001/Nachi%20Taisha.htm|url-status = dead}}</ref>
Nachi-no-Hi Matsuri Fire Festival, performed on {{ill|July 14|en|July 14|WD=Q2691|lt=July 14}}, is the major festival of Kumano Nachi Taisha. It is a fire festival in which six-meter-high portable shrines symbolically representing the purification of the waterfall with the fires from oversized torches is laboriously carried by men dressed in white.<ref>{{Cite web|url = http://www.tb-kumano.jp/en/world-heritage/kumano-nachi-taisha/|title = Kumano Nachi Taisha 熊野那智大社|access-date = October 9, 2014|website = Sacred Kumano|publisher = Tanabe City Kumano Tourism Bureau}}</ref>
==See also== * {{ill|List of Shinto shrines|en|List of Shinto shrines|WD=Q120679573|lt=List of Shinto shrines}} * {{ill|Twenty-Two Shrines|en|Twenty-Two Shrines|WD=Q215320|lt=Twenty-Two Shrines}} * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]

The [[Kumano Kodō]] route connects it to other sites under the same classification, which are primarily located in {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314|lt=Wakayama Prefecture}}, Japan. The four sites on the route, classified as pilgrimage destinations and World Heritage Sites, are: 1) Nachi Taisha; 2) [[Kumano Hongū Taisha|Hongū Taisha]]; 3) [[Kumano Hayatama Taisha|Hayatama Taisha]]; 4) {{ill|Mount Kōya|en|Mount Kōya|WD=Q535065|lt=Koya-san}}.
Kumano Nachi Taisha is also one of the three sacred [[Kumano shrine|Kumano Sanzan]] shrines: # Kumano Nachi Taisha # [[Kumano Hongū Taisha]] # [[Kumano Hayatama Taisha]] This classification is based mostly in Japanese history, as pilgrims would travel to all three sites to complete their pilgrimage.
Kumano Nachi Taisha is an example of Buddhist and Shinto {{ill|Syncretism|en|Syncretism|WD=Q172904|lt=Syncretism}} ([[Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu shūgō]]) nestled in the Kii Mountains, near Kii Katsuura, Japan. Cedar forests surround the site. The 133-meter [[Nachi Falls|Nachi Waterfall]], worshiped at the Hiryū Shrine near Kumano Nachi Taisha, is believed to be inhabited by a ''[[kami]]'' called Hiryū [[Gongen]].<ref name="nachi">{{cite web|url=http://www.kkr.mlit.go.jp/wakayama/rekishi/disco131.html|title=Hiryū Gongen|last=Kamizaka|first=Jirō|publisher=Ministry of Land, Infrastructure and Transport - Kinki Regional Development Bureau|language=ja|access-date=28 March 2010|archive-date=23 July 2011|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110723162553/http://www.kkr.mlit.go.jp/wakayama/rekishi/disco131.html|url-status=dead}}</ref> Also, there is a sacred tree at this site, the Sacred [[:en:Camphor laurel|Camphor Tree]], located between the Nachi Shrine (''[[Heiden (Shinto)|heiden]]'') and [[Seiganto-ji|Seigantoji temple]]. It is 850 years old and is said to have been planted by {{ill|Taira no Shigemori|en|Taira no Shigemori|WD=Q1184985|lt=Taira-no-Shigemori}} (1138–1179). The straw rope (''{{ill|Shimenawa|en|Shimenawa|WD=Q1887421|lt=Shimenawa}}'') and paper flags show that this tree has been sanctified as a ''kami''. The tree is alive with moss and ferns and other small plants growing on its ancient limbs. It is possible to enter the base of the tree, where there is a small altar for making offerings, and come out above ground level.<ref>{{Cite web|url = http://sacredjapan.com/Temple%2001/Nachi%20Taisha.htm|title = Nachi Taisha-Sacred Camphor Tree|access-date = October 9, 2014|website = Sacred Japan|last = Juno|first = Cate Kodo|archive-date = March 3, 2016|archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20160303212124/http://sacredjapan.com/Temple%2001/Nachi%20Taisha.htm|url-status = dead}}</ref>
Nachi-no-Hi Matsuri Fire Festival, performed on {{ill|July 14|en|July 14|WD=Q2691|lt=July 14}}, is the major festival of Kumano Nachi Taisha. It is a fire festival in which six-meter-high portable shrines symbolically representing the purification of the waterfall with the fires from oversized torches is laboriously carried by men dressed in white.<ref>{{Cite web|url = http://www.tb-kumano.jp/en/world-heritage/kumano-nachi-taisha/|title = Kumano Nachi Taisha 熊野那智大社|access-date = October 9, 2014|website = Sacred Kumano|publisher = Tanabe City Kumano Tourism Bureau}}</ref>
==See also== * {{ill|List of Shinto shrines|en|List of Shinto shrines|WD=Q120679573|lt=List of Shinto shrines}} * {{ill|Twenty-Two Shrines|en|Twenty-Two Shrines|WD=Q215320|lt=Twenty-Two Shrines}} * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kumano-Nachi-shrine]][[de:Kumano Nachi-Taisha]][[el:Κουμάνο Νάτσι Τάισα]][[en:Kumano Nachi Taisha]][[eu:Kumano Nachi Taisha]][[fa:کومانو ناچی تایشا]][[fr:Kumano Nachi-taisha]][[it:Kumano Nachi Taisha]][[ja:熊野那智大社]][[la:Kumano Nachi Taisha]][[lt:Načio maldykla]][[ru:Кумано нати тайся]][[tr:Kumano Nachi-Taisha]][[zh:熊野那智大社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kumano-Nachi-shrine]][[de:Kumano Nachi-Taisha]][[el:Κουμάνο Νάτσι Τάισα]][[en:Kumano Nachi Taisha]][[eu:Kumano Nachi Taisha]][[fa:کومانو ناچی تایشا]][[fr:Kumano Nachi-taisha]][[it:Kumano Nachi Taisha]][[ja:熊野那智大社]][[la:Kumano Nachi Taisha]][[lt:Načio maldykla]][[ru:Кумано нати тайся]][[tr:Kumano Nachi-Taisha]][[zh:熊野那智大社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[Category:sn_crud commons:Category:Kumano-Nachi-shrine]][[de:Kumano Nachi-Taisha]][[el:Κουμάνο Νάτσι Τάισα]][[en:Kumano Nachi Taisha]][[eu:Kumano Nachi Taisha]][[fa:کومانو ناچی تایشا]][[fr:Kumano Nachi-taisha]][[it:Kumano Nachi Taisha]][[ja:熊野那智大社]][[la:Kumano Nachi Taisha]][[lt:Načio maldykla]][[ru:Кумано нати тайся]][[tr:Kumano Nachi-Taisha]][[zh:熊野那智大社]]
{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Nachikatsuura, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = {{Nihongo|Kumano Nachi Taisha|熊野那智大社}} | image = Kumano-Nachi-shrine002.JPG | caption = Kumano Nachi Taisha's ''[[Heiden (Shinto)|heiden]]'' | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|33|40|07|N|135|53|26|E|display=title,inline|region:JP_type:landmark_source:dewiki}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Fusumi no kami (Izanami no mikoto) and others<ref>Cali, Joseph. Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion. — Honolulu, 2013. — pp. 253-256.</ref> | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = {{ill|Nachikatsuura|en|Nachikatsuura|WD=Q1346730|lt=Nachikatsuura|1=Nachikatsuura}}, [[Higashimuro District, Wakayama|Higashimuro District]], {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314|lt=Wakayama Prefecture|1=Wakayama Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}} | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }}
{{Nihongo|'''Kumano Nachi Taisha'''|熊野那智大社}} is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} and part of the {{ill|UNESCO|en|UNESCO|WD=Q7809|lt=UNESCO|1=UNESCO}}-designated [[:en:World Heritage|World Heritage]] {{ill|Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range|en|Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range|WD=Q611345|lt=Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range|1=Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. The [[Kumano Kodō]] route connects it to other sites under the same classification, which are primarily located in {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314|lt=Wakayama Prefecture|1=Wakayama Prefecture}}, Japan. The four sites on the route, classified as pilgrimage destinations and World Heritage Sites, are: 1) Nachi Taisha; 2) [[Kumano Hongū Taisha|Hongū Taisha]]; 3) [[Kumano Hayatama Taisha|Hayatama Taisha]]; 4) {{ill|Mount Kōya|en|Mount Kōya|WD=Q535065|lt=Koya-san|1=Mount Kōya}}.
Kumano Nachi Taisha is also one of the three sacred [[Kumano shrine|Kumano Sanzan]] shrines: # Kumano Nachi Taisha # [[Kumano Hongū Taisha]] # [[Kumano Hayatama Taisha]] This classification is based mostly in Japanese history, as pilgrims would travel to all three sites to complete their pilgrimage.
Kumano Nachi Taisha is an example of Buddhist and Shinto {{ill|Syncretism|en|Syncretism|WD=Q172904|lt=Syncretism|1=Syncretism}} ([[Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu shūgō]]) nestled in the Kii Mountains, near Kii Katsuura, Japan. Cedar forests surround the site. The 133-meter [[Nachi Falls|Nachi Waterfall]], worshiped at the Hiryū Shrine near Kumano Nachi Taisha, is believed to be inhabited by a ''[[kami]]'' called Hiryū [[Gongen]].<ref name="nachi">{{cite web|url=http://www.kkr.mlit.go.jp/wakayama/rekishi/disco131.html|title=Hiryū Gongen|last=Kamizaka|first=Jirō|publisher=Ministry of Land, Infrastructure and Transport - Kinki Regional Development Bureau|language=ja|access-date=28 March 2010|archive-date=23 July 2011|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110723162553/http://www.kkr.mlit.go.jp/wakayama/rekishi/disco131.html|url-status=dead}}</ref> Also, there is a sacred tree at this site, the Sacred [[:en:Camphor laurel|Camphor Tree]], located between the Nachi Shrine (''[[Heiden (Shinto)|heiden]]'') and [[Seiganto-ji|Seigantoji temple]]. It is 850 years old and is said to have been planted by {{ill|Taira no Shigemori|en|Taira no Shigemori|WD=Q1184985|lt=Taira-no-Shigemori|1=Taira no Shigemori}} (1138–1179). The straw rope (''{{ill|Shimenawa|en|Shimenawa|WD=Q1887421|lt=Shimenawa|1=Shimenawa}}'') and paper flags show that this tree has been sanctified as a ''kami''. The tree is alive with moss and ferns and other small plants growing on its ancient limbs. It is possible to enter the base of the tree, where there is a small altar for making offerings, and come out above ground level.<ref>{{Cite web|url = http://sacredjapan.com/Temple%2001/Nachi%20Taisha.htm|title = Nachi Taisha-Sacred Camphor Tree|access-date = October 9, 2014|website = Sacred Japan|last = Juno|first = Cate Kodo|archive-date = March 3, 2016|archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20160303212124/http://sacredjapan.com/Temple%2001/Nachi%20Taisha.htm|url-status = dead}}</ref>
Nachi-no-Hi Matsuri Fire Festival, performed on {{ill|July 14|en|July 14|WD=Q2691|lt=July 14|1=July 14}}, is the major festival of Kumano Nachi Taisha. It is a fire festival in which six-meter-high portable shrines symbolically representing the purification of the waterfall with the fires from oversized torches is laboriously carried by men dressed in white.<ref>{{Cite web|url = http://www.tb-kumano.jp/en/world-heritage/kumano-nachi-taisha/|title = Kumano Nachi Taisha 熊野那智大社|access-date = October 9, 2014|website = Sacred Kumano|publisher = Tanabe City Kumano Tourism Bureau}}</ref>

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Nachikatsuura, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = {{Nihongo|Kumano Nachi Taisha|熊野那智大社}} | image = Kumano-Nachi-shrine002.JPG | caption = Kumano Nachi Taisha's ''[[Heiden (Shinto)|heiden]]'' | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|33|40|07|N|135|53|26|E|display=title,inline|region:JP_type:landmark_source:dewiki}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Fusumi no kami (Izanami no mikoto) and others<ref>Cali, Joseph. Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion. — Honolulu, 2013. — pp. 253-256.</ref> | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = {{ill|Nachikatsuura|en|Nachikatsuura|WD=Q1346730|lt=Nachikatsuura|1=Nachikatsuura}}, [[Higashimuro District, Wakayama|Higashimuro District]], {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314|lt=Wakayama Prefecture|1=Wakayama Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}} | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }}
{{Nihongo|'''Kumano Nachi Taisha'''|熊野那智大社}} is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} and part of the {{ill|UNESCO|en|UNESCO|WD=Q7809|lt=UNESCO|1=UNESCO}}-designated [[:en:World Heritage|World Heritage]] {{ill|Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range|en|Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range|WD=Q611345|lt=Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range|1=Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. The [[Kumano Kodō]] route connects it to other sites under the same classification, which are primarily located in {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314|lt=Wakayama Prefecture|1=Wakayama Prefecture}}, Japan. The four sites on the route, classified as pilgrimage destinations and World Heritage Sites, are: 1) Nachi Taisha; 2) [[Kumano Hongū Taisha|Hongū Taisha]]; 3) [[Kumano Hayatama Taisha|Hayatama Taisha]]; 4) {{ill|Mount Kōya|en|Mount Kōya|WD=Q535065|lt=Koya-san|1=Mount Kōya}}.
Kumano Nachi Taisha is also one of the three sacred [[Kumano shrine|Kumano Sanzan]] shrines: # Kumano Nachi Taisha # [[Kumano Hongū Taisha]] # [[Kumano Hayatama Taisha]] This classification is based mostly in Japanese history, as pilgrims would travel to all three sites to complete their pilgrimage.
Kumano Nachi Taisha is an example of Buddhist and Shinto {{ill|Syncretism|en|Syncretism|WD=Q172904|lt=Syncretism|1=Syncretism}} ([[Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu shūgō]]) nestled in the Kii Mountains, near Kii Katsuura, Japan. Cedar forests surround the site. The 133-meter [[Nachi Falls|Nachi Waterfall]], worshiped at the Hiryū Shrine near Kumano Nachi Taisha, is believed to be inhabited by a ''[[kami]]'' called Hiryū [[Gongen]].<ref name="nachi">{{cite web|url=http://www.kkr.mlit.go.jp/wakayama/rekishi/disco131.html|title=Hiryū Gongen|last=Kamizaka|first=Jirō|publisher=Ministry of Land, Infrastructure and Transport - Kinki Regional Development Bureau|language=ja|access-date=28 March 2010|archive-date=23 July 2011|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110723162553/http://www.kkr.mlit.go.jp/wakayama/rekishi/disco131.html|url-status=dead}}</ref> Also, there is a sacred tree at this site, the Sacred [[:en:Camphor laurel|Camphor Tree]], located between the Nachi Shrine (''[[Heiden (Shinto)|heiden]]'') and [[Seiganto-ji|Seigantoji temple]]. It is 850 years old and is said to have been planted by {{ill|Taira no Shigemori|en|Taira no Shigemori|WD=Q1184985|lt=Taira-no-Shigemori|1=Taira no Shigemori}} (1138–1179). The straw rope (''{{ill|Shimenawa|en|Shimenawa|WD=Q1887421|lt=Shimenawa|1=Shimenawa}}'') and paper flags show that this tree has been sanctified as a ''kami''. The tree is alive with moss and ferns and other small plants growing on its ancient limbs. It is possible to enter the base of the tree, where there is a small altar for making offerings, and come out above ground level.<ref>{{Cite web|url = http://sacredjapan.com/Temple%2001/Nachi%20Taisha.htm|title = Nachi Taisha-Sacred Camphor Tree|access-date = October 9, 2014|website = Sacred Japan|last = Juno|first = Cate Kodo|archive-date = March 3, 2016|archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20160303212124/http://sacredjapan.com/Temple%2001/Nachi%20Taisha.htm|url-status = dead}}</ref>
Nachi-no-Hi Matsuri Fire Festival, performed on {{ill|July 14|en|July 14|WD=Q2691|lt=July 14|1=July 14}}, is the major festival of Kumano Nachi Taisha. It is a fire festival in which six-meter-high portable shrines symbolically representing the purification of the waterfall with the fires from oversized torches is laboriously carried by men dressed in white.<ref>{{Cite web|url = http://www.tb-kumano.jp/en/world-heritage/kumano-nachi-taisha/|title = Kumano Nachi Taisha 熊野那智大社|access-date = October 9, 2014|website = Sacred Kumano|publisher = Tanabe City Kumano Tourism Bureau}}</ref>

The [[Kumano Kodō]] route connects it to other sites under the same classification, which are primarily located in {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314|lt=Wakayama Prefecture|1=Wakayama Prefecture}}, Japan. The four sites on the route, classified as pilgrimage destinations and World Heritage Sites, are: 1) Nachi Taisha; 2) [[Kumano Hongū Taisha|Hongū Taisha]]; 3) [[Kumano Hayatama Taisha|Hayatama Taisha]]; 4) {{ill|Mount Kōya|en|Mount Kōya|WD=Q535065|lt=Koya-san|1=Mount Kōya}}.
Kumano Nachi Taisha is also one of the three sacred [[Kumano shrine|Kumano Sanzan]] shrines: # Kumano Nachi Taisha # [[Kumano Hongū Taisha]] # [[Kumano Hayatama Taisha]] This classification is based mostly in Japanese history, as pilgrims would travel to all three sites to complete their pilgrimage.
Kumano Nachi Taisha is an example of Buddhist and Shinto {{ill|Syncretism|en|Syncretism|WD=Q172904|lt=Syncretism|1=Syncretism}} ([[Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu shūgō]]) nestled in the Kii Mountains, near Kii Katsuura, Japan. Cedar forests surround the site. The 133-meter [[Nachi Falls|Nachi Waterfall]], worshiped at the Hiryū Shrine near Kumano Nachi Taisha, is believed to be inhabited by a ''[[kami]]'' called Hiryū [[Gongen]].<ref name="nachi">{{cite web|url=http://www.kkr.mlit.go.jp/wakayama/rekishi/disco131.html|title=Hiryū Gongen|last=Kamizaka|first=Jirō|publisher=Ministry of Land, Infrastructure and Transport - Kinki Regional Development Bureau|language=ja|access-date=28 March 2010|archive-date=23 July 2011|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110723162553/http://www.kkr.mlit.go.jp/wakayama/rekishi/disco131.html|url-status=dead}}</ref> Also, there is a sacred tree at this site, the Sacred [[:en:Camphor laurel|Camphor Tree]], located between the Nachi Shrine (''[[Heiden (Shinto)|heiden]]'') and [[Seiganto-ji|Seigantoji temple]]. It is 850 years old and is said to have been planted by {{ill|Taira no Shigemori|en|Taira no Shigemori|WD=Q1184985|lt=Taira-no-Shigemori|1=Taira no Shigemori}} (1138–1179). The straw rope (''{{ill|Shimenawa|en|Shimenawa|WD=Q1887421|lt=Shimenawa|1=Shimenawa}}'') and paper flags show that this tree has been sanctified as a ''kami''. The tree is alive with moss and ferns and other small plants growing on its ancient limbs. It is possible to enter the base of the tree, where there is a small altar for making offerings, and come out above ground level.<ref>{{Cite web|url = http://sacredjapan.com/Temple%2001/Nachi%20Taisha.htm|title = Nachi Taisha-Sacred Camphor Tree|access-date = October 9, 2014|website = Sacred Japan|last = Juno|first = Cate Kodo|archive-date = March 3, 2016|archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20160303212124/http://sacredjapan.com/Temple%2001/Nachi%20Taisha.htm|url-status = dead}}</ref>
Nachi-no-Hi Matsuri Fire Festival, performed on {{ill|July 14|en|July 14|WD=Q2691|lt=July 14|1=July 14}}, is the major festival of Kumano Nachi Taisha. It is a fire festival in which six-meter-high portable shrines symbolically representing the purification of the waterfall with the fires from oversized torches is laboriously carried by men dressed in white.<ref>{{Cite web|url = http://www.tb-kumano.jp/en/world-heritage/kumano-nachi-taisha/|title = Kumano Nachi Taisha 熊野那智大社|access-date = October 9, 2014|website = Sacred Kumano|publisher = Tanabe City Kumano Tourism Bureau}}</ref>
==See also== * {{ill|List of Shinto shrines|en|List of Shinto shrines|WD=Q120679573|lt=List of Shinto shrines|1=List of Shinto shrines}} * {{ill|Twenty-Two Shrines|en|Twenty-Two Shrines|WD=Q215320|lt=Twenty-Two Shrines|1=Twenty-Two Shrines}} * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]

==External links== *[http://www.tb-kumano.jp/en/index.html#2 Tanabe City Kumano Tourism Bureau] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110925191301/http://www.tb-kumano.jp/en/index.html#2 |date=2011-09-25 }} *[http://www.kumadoco.net/kodo_eng/about/index.html#no2 Description of the sites] **[https://web.archive.org/web/20110610222210/http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/sekaiisan/english/e-kumano_si.html Wakayama Prefecture webpage about Kumano Nachi Taisha] *[http://www.nachikan.jp/en/ Nachi-Katsuura Town Tourism Association]

{{Nihongo|'''Kumano Nachi Taisha'''|熊野那智大社}} is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} and part of the {{ill|UNESCO|en|UNESCO|WD=Q7809|lt=UNESCO|1=UNESCO}}-designated [[:en:World Heritage|World Heritage]] {{ill|Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range|en|Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range|WD=Q611345|lt=Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range|1=Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. The [[Kumano Kodō]] route connects it to other sites under the same classification, which are primarily located in {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314|lt=Wakayama Prefecture|1=Wakayama Prefecture}}, Japan. The four sites on the route, classified as pilgrimage destinations and World Heritage Sites, are: 1) Nachi Taisha; 2) [[Kumano Hongū Taisha|Hongū Taisha]]; 3) [[Kumano Hayatama Taisha|Hayatama Taisha]]; 4) {{ill|Mount Kōya|en|Mount Kōya|WD=Q535065|lt=Koya-san|1=Mount Kōya}}.

Kumano Nachi Taisha is an example of Buddhist and Shinto {{ill|Syncretism|en|Syncretism|WD=Q172904|lt=Syncretism|1=Syncretism}} ([[Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu shūgō]]) nestled in the Kii Mountains, near Kii Katsuura, Japan. {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Nachikatsuura, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = {{Nihongo|Kumano Nachi Taisha|熊野那智大社}} | image = Kumano-Nachi-shrine002.JPG | caption = Kumano Nachi Taisha's ''[[Heiden (Shinto)|heiden]]'' | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|33|40|07|N|135|53|26|E|display=title,inline|region:JP_type:landmark_source:dewiki}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Fusumi no kami (Izanami no mikoto) and others<ref>Cali, Joseph. Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion. — Honolulu, 2013. — pp. 253-256.</ref> | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = {{ill|Nachikatsuura|en|Nachikatsuura|qid=Q1346730|lt=Nachikatsuura|1=Nachikatsuura}}, [[Higashimuro District, Wakayama|Higashimuro District]], {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|qid=Q131314|lt=Wakayama Prefecture|1=Wakayama Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}} | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }}
{{Nihongo|'''Kumano Nachi Taisha'''|熊野那智大社}} is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} and part of the {{ill|UNESCO|en|UNESCO|qid=Q7809|lt=UNESCO|1=UNESCO}}-designated [[:en:World Heritage|World Heritage]] {{ill|Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range|en|Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range|qid=Q611345|lt=Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range|1=Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. The [[Kumano Kodō]] route connects it to other sites under the same classification, which are primarily located in {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|qid=Q131314|lt=Wakayama Prefecture|1=Wakayama Prefecture}}, Japan. The four sites on the route, classified as pilgrimage destinations and World Heritage Sites, are: 1) Nachi Taisha; 2) [[Kumano Hongū Taisha|Hongū Taisha]]; 3) [[Kumano Hayatama Taisha|Hayatama Taisha]]; 4) {{ill|Mount Kōya|en|Mount Kōya|qid=Q535065|lt=Koya-san|1=Mount Kōya}}.
Kumano Nachi Taisha is also one of the three sacred [[Kumano shrine|Kumano Sanzan]] shrines: # Kumano Nachi Taisha # [[Kumano Hongū Taisha]] # [[Kumano Hayatama Taisha]] This classification is based mostly in Japanese history, as pilgrims would travel to all three sites to complete their pilgrimage.
Kumano Nachi Taisha is an example of Buddhist and Shinto {{ill|Syncretism|en|Syncretism|qid=Q172904|lt=Syncretism|1=Syncretism}} ([[Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu shūgō]]) nestled in the Kii Mountains, near Kii Katsuura, Japan. Cedar forests surround the site. The 133-meter [[Nachi Falls|Nachi Waterfall]], worshiped at the Hiryū Shrine near Kumano Nachi Taisha, is believed to be inhabited by a ''[[kami]]'' called Hiryū [[Gongen]].<ref name="nachi">{{cite web|url=http://www.kkr.mlit.go.jp/wakayama/rekishi/disco131.html|title=Hiryū Gongen|last=Kamizaka|first=Jirō|publisher=Ministry of Land, Infrastructure and Transport - Kinki Regional Development Bureau|language=ja|access-date=28 March 2010|archive-date=23 July 2011|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110723162553/http://www.kkr.mlit.go.jp/wakayama/rekishi/disco131.html|url-status=dead}}</ref> Also, there is a sacred tree at this site, the Sacred [[:en:Camphor laurel|Camphor Tree]], located between the Nachi Shrine (''[[Heiden (Shinto)|heiden]]'') and [[Seiganto-ji|Seigantoji temple]]. It is 850 years old and is said to have been planted by {{ill|Taira no Shigemori|en|Taira no Shigemori|qid=Q1184985|lt=Taira-no-Shigemori|1=Taira no Shigemori}} (1138–1179). The straw rope (''{{ill|Shimenawa|en|Shimenawa|qid=Q1887421|lt=Shimenawa|1=Shimenawa}}'') and paper flags show that this tree has been sanctified as a ''kami''. The tree is alive with moss and ferns and other small plants growing on its ancient limbs. It is possible to enter the base of the tree, where there is a small altar for making offerings, and come out above ground level.<ref>{{Cite web|url = http://sacredjapan.com/Temple%2001/Nachi%20Taisha.htm|title = Nachi Taisha-Sacred Camphor Tree|access-date = October 9, 2014|website = Sacred Japan|last = Juno|first = Cate Kodo|archive-date = March 3, 2016|archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20160303212124/http://sacredjapan.com/Temple%2001/Nachi%20Taisha.htm|url-status = dead}}</ref>
Nachi-no-Hi Matsuri Fire Festival, performed on {{ill|July 14|en|July 14|qid=Q2691|lt=July 14|1=July 14}}, is the major festival of Kumano Nachi Taisha. It is a fire festival in which six-meter-high portable shrines symbolically representing the purification of the waterfall with the fires from oversized torches is laboriously carried by men dressed in white.<ref>{{Cite web|url = http://www.tb-kumano.jp/en/world-heritage/kumano-nachi-taisha/|title = Kumano Nachi Taisha 熊野那智大社|access-date = October 9, 2014|website = Sacred Kumano|publisher = Tanabe City Kumano Tourism Bureau}}</ref>
==See also== * {{ill|List of Shinto shrines|en|List of Shinto shrines|qid=Q120679573|lt=List of Shinto shrines|1=List of Shinto shrines}} * {{ill|Twenty-Two Shrines|en|Twenty-Two Shrines|qid=Q215320|lt=Twenty-Two Shrines|1=Twenty-Two Shrines}} * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]

==External links== *[http://www.tb-kumano.jp/en/index.html#2 Tanabe City Kumano Tourism Bureau] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110925191301/http://www.tb-kumano.jp/en/index.html#2 |date=2011-09-25 }} *[http://www.kumadoco.net/kodo_eng/about/index.html#no2 Description of the sites] **[https://web.archive.org/web/20110610222210/http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/sekaiisan/english/e-kumano_si.html Wakayama Prefecture webpage about Kumano Nachi Taisha] *[http://www.nachikan.jp/en/ Nachi-Katsuura Town Tourism Association]
